<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1: POLYPHENYLENE SULPHIDE (PPS) — KTU COMPREHENSIVE NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **FORMAL KTU 2024 SYLLABUS DEFINITION**
> **Polyphenylene Sulphide (PPS)** is a **semi-crystalline, high-performance engineering thermoplastic** whose repeating constitutional unit consists of a *para*-substituted phenylene ring linked through a sulphide (–S–) bridge. Its linear chemical structure is represented as **–[–C₆H₄–S–]ₙ–**, where **n** is the degree of polymerization. PPS is one of the most commercially significant members of the poly(arylene sulphide) family and is renowned for its intrinsic flame retardancy, exceptional chemical inertness, and high thermal stability.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chain of rigid, hexagonal benzene "locks"** (phenylene rings), each one connected to the next by a **single, flexible "hinge" made of a sulfur atom**. This alternating arrangement of **rigid aromatic rings + flexible sulfur bridges** is the molecular secret of PPS:
- The **aromatic rings** provide strength, heat resistance, and stiffness (like steel girders in a building).
- The **sulphur bridges** provide a small amount of flexibility, allowing the polymer to be processed as a melt.
- The resulting chain is so thermally stable and chemically inert that PPS is often called the **"steel of the plastics world."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> PPS is classified as a **Specialty/High-Performance Polymer** because it retains useful mechanical and electrical properties continuously at temperatures exceeding **200 °C**, even in aggressive chemical environments where conventional plastics (PE, PP, PS) would fail or melt.

### Fundamental Identity Card

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Repeating Unit | **–C₆H₄–S–** (*para*-linked) |
| IUPAC Name | Poly(1,4-phenylene sulphide) |
| CAS Number | **26125-40-6** |
| Polymer Class | Poly(arylene sulphide) |
| Tacticity | Atactic, semi-crystalline |
| Trade Names | Ryton®, Torelina®, Fortron®, DIC.PPS® |
| Density | **1.35 g/cm³** |
| Colour (Virgin) | White to pale beige |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Molecular Architecture — Why PPS Behaves the Way It Does

PPS is built from a deceptively simple, perfectly alternating structure. The following logical chain explains the **"Why"** behind every property:

1. **Fully Aromatic Backbone with Para-Linkage**
   The *para* (1,4) connection of the benzene ring means the polymer chain is **linear and highly symmetric**.
   - *Why it matters:* Symmetry promotes efficient chain packing → crystallinity → high mechanical strength and high melting point.

2. **Presence of the Thioether (–S–) Linkage**
   Sulfur is divalent, but the C–S–C bond angle (~105°) introduces a small "kink" and a degree of rotational freedom.
   - *Why it matters:* Allows melt-processing (unlike fully rigid ladder polymers) while still maintaining strong resonance stabilization from the lone pairs of sulfur with the aromatic π-system.

3. **High Bond Dissociation Energy of C–S and C–C (aromatic) Bonds**
   The aromatic C–C bond has a dissociation energy of **~610 kJ/mol**, and C–S bonds contribute additional stability.
   - *Why it matters:* PPS resists thermal degradation up to **~450 °C** in inert atmospheres and offers exceptional long-term heat aging.

4. **Lack of Hydrolyzable or Polar Functional Groups**
   PPS contains only C, H, and S atoms.
   - *Why it matters:* No water absorption (≤ 0.02 %), excellent electrical insulation, and resistance to acids, bases, and organic solvents below ~200 °C.

5. **Intrinsic Flame Retardancy**
   The C–S bond liberates sulfur-containing radicals during combustion, which act as **radical scavengers in the flame zone**, terminating the chain reaction of fire propagation.
   - *Why it matters:* PPS achieves **UL-94 V-0** rating (highest flame resistance) without any flame-retardant additives.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Data Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all equations, parameters, and standard values** a KTU student must memorize for the ESE/IA evaluation. Every symbol and unit is exam-ready.

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeating unit MW | $M_0 = 6(12) + 4(1) + 32 = 108$ g/mol | **108 g/mol** | Used to compute $\overline{M_n}$ from degree of polymerization |
| Degree of polymerization | $DP_n = \dfrac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$ | 100 – 500 | Determines mechanical strength & melt viscosity |
| Number-average MW | $\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | 10 000 – 50 000 g/mol | Trade name selection |
| Weight-average MW | $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | 40 000 – 200 000 g/mol | Polydispersity index calculation |
| Polydispersity Index | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$ | 2 – 4 for PPS | Indicates MW distribution breadth |
| Glass transition (Tg) | Empirical | **85 – 90 °C** | Upper use limit in amorphous regions |
| Melting point (Tm) | DSC peak | **280 – 290 °C** | Processing temperature target |
| Crystallinity (Xc) | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\%$ | 30 – 65 % | Governs stiffness and chemical resistance |
| Heat deflection temp (HDT) | ASTM D648 (1.82 MPa) | **260 °C** | Continuous service temperature |
| Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI) | ASTM D2863 | **34 – 44 %** | Self-extinguishing threshold |
| Tensile strength | ASTM D638 | 70 – 80 MPa | Mechanical load capacity |
| Dielectric strength | ASTM D149 | 15 – 25 kV/mm | Electrical insulation rating |
| Water absorption (24 h) | ASTM D570 | **0.02 %** | Dimensional stability in humid service |
| Decomposition temp | TGA, 5 % wt loss in air | **500 – 510 °C** | Long-term thermal ceiling |
| Melt viscosity (at 300 °C) | Capillary rheometry | 100 – 10 000 Pa·s | Processing window indicator |
| Tensile modulus | ASTM D638 | 3.3 – 4.0 GPa | Stiffness comparable to short-fiber composites |

> **Engineering Utility Snapshot:** The high **LOI (34–44 %)** combined with the absence of halogenated flame retardants makes PPS the polymer of choice for **EV battery housings, aerospace interiors, and high-voltage connectors**, where both flame safety and electrical insulation are non-negotiable.

### 2.3 Crystallinity and Cross-linking — Two Critical KTU Concepts

PPS exists in **three structural states**, each with distinct properties:

- **Linear (Virgin) PPS** — soluble, melt-processible thermoplastic.
- **Cured / Cross-linked PPS** — formed by heating in air (250 °C, several hours). The oxygen bridges adjacent chains, creating a **thermoset** with dramatically improved mechanical strength, chemical resistance, and thermal stability (heat deflection up to **>260 °C**).
- **Filled PPS** — reinforced with glass fibers, carbon fibers, or mineral fillers for engineering applications.

The **cross-linking reaction** is an oxidative process:

$$\text{2 (–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–S–)} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–S–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–S(O)–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This introduces new **C–C and S–O bonds** between chains, locking them together.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Illustrations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How to Calculate the Degree of Polymerization (DP) for a Given Molecular Weight

**Problem statement (KTU-style):** A commercial PPS sample has a number-average molecular weight $\overline{M_n} = 32{,}400$ g/mol. Compute (a) the degree of polymerization, (b) the weight of sulfur in 1 mole of repeating units, and (c) verify the polydispersity index given $\overline{M_w} = 64{,}800$ g/mol.

#### Step 1 — Identify the molar mass of the repeating unit

The repeating unit is **–C₆H₄–S–**.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= 6 \cdot M_C + 4 \cdot M_H + 1 \cdot M_S \\[4pt]
&= 6(12.01) + 4(1.008) + 1(32.06) \;\text{g/mol} \\[4pt]
&= 72.06 + 4.032 + 32.06 \\[4pt]
&= 108.15 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Each unit contributes 6 aromatic carbons, 4 aromatic hydrogens, and 1 divalent sulfur. This is the **chemical formula mass**, not the monomer mass.

#### Step 2 — Compute the degree of polymerization

$$\begin{aligned}
DP_n &= \dfrac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{32{,}400 \;\text{g/mol}}{108.15 \;\text{g/mol}} \\[4pt]
&= 299.6 \approx 300
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** A polymer of $DP_n = 300$ contains **300 phenylene sulphide units** end-to-end, giving a chain length of roughly **150 nm** (each unit ≈ 0.5 nm along the chain axis).

#### Step 3 — Mass fraction of sulfur in PPS

$$\begin{aligned}
w_S &= \dfrac{M_S}{M_0} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{32.06}{108.15} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 29.65 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Almost **30 % of the polymer's mass is sulfur**, which explains the polymer's excellent flame retardancy and density (~1.35 g/cm³, higher than most thermoplastics).

#### Step 4 — Verify the polydispersity index

$$\begin{aligned}
PDI &= \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{64{,}800}{32{,}400} \\[4pt]
&= 2.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** A PDI of **2.00** is consistent with a step-growth polymerization (e.g., the Phillips process), which is the actual industrial route for PPS. Condensation polymers typically show PDI values of 2.0–2.5.

### 3.2 Numerical Estimate: Theoretical Crystallinity from DSC Data

**Problem:** A DSC scan of PPS gives an experimental heat of fusion $\Delta H_f = 35.2$ J/g. The literature value for 100 % crystalline PPS is $\Delta H_f^0 = 77.5$ J/g. Compute the crystallinity.

$$\begin{aligned}
X_c &= \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{35.2}{77.5} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 45.4 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** A crystallinity of ~45 % is typical for a slow-cooled or annealed PPS specimen. Higher cooling rates from the melt (e.g., injection moulding) can reduce $X_c$ to 30 %, lowering stiffness but improving ductility.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Synthesis Routes at a Glance

| Parameter | Macallum Process (1948) | Phillips Process (1967) | Modern Variants |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monomer | *p*-Dichlorobenzene + Na₂S | *p*-Dichlorobenzene + Na₂S | Thiophenol / disulphide oxidative coupling |
| Solvent | Solid-state melt | N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) | Polar aprotic / ionic liquids |
| Temperature | 250 – 350 °C (autoclave) | 200 – 280 °C | 150 – 250 °C |
| Catalyst | None (stoichiometric) | Carboxylate salts (e.g., NaOBz) | Transition-metal complexes |
| Molecular weight | Low / oligomeric | High (commercial MW) | Tailored MW |
| Branching | High | Low → linear | Controlled |
| Industrial use | Historical | **Dominant (Ryton®, Fortron®)** | Specialty / R&D |

### 3.4 Phillips Process — Reaction Stoichiometry (Analytical)

The principal industrial reaction is:

$$n\,\text{Cl–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–Cl} + n\,\text{Na}_2\text{S} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–[–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–S–]}_n\text{–} + 2n\,\text{NaCl}$$

By-product NaCl is washed out; the PPS is then melt-stabilized and pelletized.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define Polyphenylene Sulphide. Write its repeating unit and list any two characteristic properties."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full-credit response):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Polyphenylene Sulphide (PPS) is a semi-crystalline, high-performance engineering thermoplastic belonging to the poly(arylene sulphide) family, with a linear chain of *para*-substituted phenylene rings connected by sulphide (–S–) bridges.
- **Repeating unit (1 mark):** **–[–C₆H₄–S–]ₙ–**
- **Two characteristic properties (1 mark):**
  (i) Intrinsic flame retardancy (LOI = 34–44 %; UL-94 V-0 rating without additives).
  (ii) Excellent chemical resistance to almost all solvents, acids, and bases below 200 °C; water absorption < 0.02 %.

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Explain the role of the thioether (–S–) linkage in determining the properties of PPS."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The –S– linkage performs three simultaneous roles in PPS:
1. **Flexibility contributor (1 mark):** The C–S–C bond angle (~105°) and rotational freedom allow the otherwise rigid aromatic chain to be melt-processed at 280–320 °C.
2. **Resonance / electronic contributor (1 mark):** Lone pairs on sulfur delocalize into the aromatic π-system, raising bond dissociation energy and imparting high thermal stability.
3. **Flame-retardant mechanism (1 mark):** On combustion, sulfur-containing radicals quench the flame-propagating chain reactions, granting PPS its self-extinguishing behaviour.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS**
> - Do **not** confuse **PPS (Polyphenylene Sulphide)** with **PES (Polyethersulphone)** or **PSU (Polysulphone)** — they are different polymers with different linkages (–O–S(O)₂–O– vs –S–). Examiners deduct 2 marks instantly for interchange.
> - When asked about **synthesis**, the **Phillips process** is the expected industrial answer. Mentioning only the historical Macallum route without naming the modern process is treated as an incomplete answer.
> - **Tg ≈ 85–90 °C** and **Tm ≈ 280–290 °C** are *numerical* facts frequently asked. Memory slips cost at least 1 mark.
> - Always state **both** LOI value **and** UL-94 rating when discussing flame retardancy — either alone is partial credit.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Synthesis & Structure Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Choice A]**

**(a) Describe the Phillips process for the synthesis of Polyphenylene Sulphide. Write the reaction, conditions, and discuss how molecular weight is controlled. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Phillips process**, developed by Edmonds and Hill at Phillips Petroleum (1967), is the dominant industrial route to high-MW PPS.

**1. Reaction (2 marks):**
$$n\,\text{Cl–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–Cl} + n\,\text{Na}_2\text{S} \;\xrightarrow{\text{NMP, 200–280 °C}}\; \text{–[–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–S–]}_n\text{–} + 2n\,\text{NaCl}$$

**2. Process Conditions (2 marks):**
- Solvent: **N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP)** — a polar aprotic solvent that dissolves both monomers and the growing PPS chain.
- Temperature: **200 – 280 °C**.
- Pressure: slightly above atmospheric (in an inert N₂ blanket to prevent oxidative cross-linking during synthesis).
- Catalyst: a small amount of **sodium carboxylate** (e.g., sodium benzoate, NaOBz) is added to control the rate and suppress side reactions.
- Reaction time: 5–10 hours.

**3. Molecular Weight Control (2 marks):**
- **Stoichiometry control:** A slight excess of *p*-dichlorobenzene (or of Na₂S) introduces end-groups that terminate the chain. The **Carothers equation** governs the DP:
$$DP_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$
where *r* is the stoichiometric imbalance ratio and *p* is the extent of reaction.
- **Reaction time control:** Longer reaction times shift *p* → 1, raising $DP_n$ exponentially.
- **Quenching with a monofunctional terminator** (e.g., methyl chloride) caps chain ends.

**4. After-treatment (1 mark):**
The crude product is washed with hot water to remove **NaCl by-product**, dried, and melt-extruded into pellets. Optional curing (250 °C in air) cross-links the polymer for thermoset applications.

---

**(b) Explain the relationship between the chemical structure of PPS and its (i) thermal stability, (ii) flame retardancy, and (iii) chemical resistance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Thermal Stability (2.5 marks):**
- The **fully aromatic backbone** is dominated by C–C bonds of very high bond dissociation energy (~610 kJ/mol for aromatic C–C, ~272 kJ/mol for C–S).
- Resonance stabilization of the aromatic π-system delocalizes thermal energy.
- Result: 5 % weight loss occurs only at **~500 °C in air**, and continuous service temperature is **200 – 240 °C** (HDT up to 260 °C for filled grades).

**(ii) Flame Retardancy (2.5 marks):**
- On heating, the C–S bond breaks, generating **sulphur-containing radicals (S•, SH•)**.
- These radicals act as **chain terminators** in the combustion radical chain (analogous to HBr from brominated flame retardants, but without halogen toxicity).
- The high **LOI of 34–44 %** means PPS self-extinguishes in normal atmospheric oxygen.
- Result: **UL-94 V-0** rating is achieved **without any flame-retardant additives**, an environmental and regulatory advantage.

**(iii) Chemical Resistance (2 marks):**
- The polymer chain is **non-polar and free of hydrolyzable groups** (no esters, amides, or C–O bonds).
- Strong **aromatic + thioether** backbone resists attack by acids, bases, salts, fuels, and most organic solvents below 200 °C.
- The **high degree of crystallinity (30–65 %)** prevents small molecules from diffusing into the amorphous regions.
- Result: PPS is attacked only by strong oxidizing acids (conc. HNO₃, hot H₂SO₄) and certain chlorinated solvents at high temperatures.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Properties & Applications Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Choice B]**

**(a) Tabulate the key physical, thermal, mechanical, and electrical properties of PPS. Explain how these properties make it suitable for automotive and electronic applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Property Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Test Method | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Density | ASTM D792 | 1.35 g/cm³ |
| Tensile Strength | ASTM D638 | 70 – 80 MPa |
| Tensile Modulus | ASTM D638 | 3.3 – 4.0 GPa |
| Elongation at break | ASTM D638 | 1 – 4 % (brittle) |
| Glass transition, Tg | DSC | 85 – 90 °C |
| Melting point, Tm | DSC | 280 – 290 °C |
| Heat Deflection Temp (1.82 MPa) | ASTM D648 | 260 °C |
| Continuous service temperature | — | 200 – 240 °C |
| Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI) | ASTM D2863 | 34 – 44 % |
| UL-94 Flammability | — | V-0 |
| Water absorption (24 h) | ASTM D570 | 0.02 % |
| Dielectric strength | ASTM D149 | 15 – 25 kV/mm |
| Volume resistivity | ASTM D257 | 10¹⁶ Ω·cm |
| Decomposition (5 % wt loss, air) | TGA | 500 – 510 °C |

**Application Justification (4 marks):**

**Automotive (2 marks):**
- HDT of 260 °C and continuous service at 200 °C+ allow PPS to be used in **under-the-hood components** (e.g., coolant reservoirs, fuel system parts, throttle bodies) where temperatures exceed 150 °C.
- Excellent **chemical resistance to fuels, oils, and coolants** ensures long-term dimensional and mechanical stability.
- High **dimensional stability** (low water absorption + low creep) is critical for tight-tolerance parts.

**Electronics (2 marks):**
- The combination of **flame retardancy (V-0) + high dielectric strength (15–25 kV/mm) + low moisture absorption (0.02 %)** makes PPS ideal for **connectors, sockets, switches, coil bobbins, and surface-mount device (SMD) housings**.
- **Reflow soldering compatibility** — PPS withstands the 260 °C peak temperature of lead-free reflow profiles without blistering or warping.
- It replaces metals and thermosets in miniaturized electronic assemblies, enabling **weight and cost reduction**.

---

**(b) Describe the curing (cross-linking) of PPS. How does curing convert a thermoplastic into a thermoset, and what property improvements result? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Background — Why Cure PPS? (1 mark)**
Linear virgin PPS is a thermoplastic, melt-processible but with moderate mechanical strength and somewhat limited long-term thermal-oxidative stability. Heat-curing converts it into a thermoset, dramatically upgrading its performance envelope for severe-service applications.

**2. Curing Conditions (1.5 marks):**
- **Atmosphere:** Air (oxygen is the active reagent).
- **Temperature:** 230 – 270 °C (typically around 250 °C).
- **Duration:** 2 – 6 hours, depending on part thickness and desired cross-link density.
- **Equipment:** Forced-air convection oven.

**3. Chemistry of Cross-linking (3 marks):**
During heating in air, oxidative reactions occur at the *ortho* and *para* positions of the phenylene ring, producing new inter-chain bonds:
- **C–C bonds** form between adjacent chains (direct ring-to-ring coupling).
- **C–S–C bridges** form by radical recombination.
- **S=O (sulfoxide) groups** appear as intermediate species.
The simplified representation:

$$\text{2 –[–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–S–]}_n\text{–} + \text{O}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{250 °C, air}}\; \text{Cross-linked network} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \uparrow$$

A three-dimensional network is gradually built, transforming the polymer from a **linear, soluble thermoplastic** into an **insoluble, infusible thermoset**.

**4. Property Improvements (1.5 marks):**

| Property | Before Curing (Linear) | After Curing (Cross-linked) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Deflection Temp | 200 – 220 °C | **260 – 270 °C** |
| Tensile strength | 70 MPa | 90 – 100 MPa |
| Chemical resistance | Excellent | **Superior (espec. to solvents at high T)** |
| Solubility | Soluble in NMP (above 200 °C) | **Insoluble / infusible** |
| Long-term heat aging | Moderate | **Excellent** |

Cured PPS is therefore preferred for **pump housings, valve seats, and hot-gas filtration fabrics** where the part must survive years of contact with aggressive chemicals at elevated temperatures.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✔ **PPS repeating unit:** **–[–C₆H₄–S–]ₙ–** (*para*-linked, semi-crystalline, aromatic + thioether).
- ✔ **Molar mass of repeating unit:** **M₀ = 108 g/mol**; sulfur mass fraction ≈ **30 %**.
- ✔ **Glass transition (Tg):** **85 – 90 °C**; **Melting point (Tm):** **280 – 290 °C**.
- ✔ **Continuous service temperature:** **200 – 240 °C**; **HDT:** up to **260 °C** (filled/cured grades).
- ✔ **Decomposition (5 % wt loss, air):** **~500 – 510 °C**.
- ✔ **LOI:** **34 – 44 %**; **UL-94 V-0** — flame retardancy is **intrinsic** (no additives needed).
- ✔ **Water absorption:** **≤ 0.02 %** — extremely low (dimensional stability).
- ✔ **Synthesis (industrial):** **Phillips process** — *p*-dichlorobenzene + Na₂S in NMP at 200–280 °C, with sodium carboxylate catalyst; by-product NaCl.
- ✔ **Carothers' equation** controls molecular weight through stoichiometric imbalance (*r*) and conversion (*p*).
- ✔ **Curing:** Heating in **air at ~250 °C for 2–6 h** → oxidative cross-linking → thermoplastic → thermoset.
- ✔ **Trade names to memorize:** **Ryton® (Chevron Phillips), Torelina® (Toray), Fortron® (Celanese), DIC.PPS®**.
- ✔ **Key application sectors:** **Automotive (under-hood), Electronics (connectors, SMD), Industrial (pumps/valves), Coatings, Hot-gas filter fabrics**.
- ✔ **Common confusion to avoid:** PPS ≠ PES ≠ PSU; only PPS has the simple –C₆H₄–S– backbone without –O– or –S(O)₂– groups.
- ✔ **PDI of step-growth PPS ≈ 2**, consistent with the Carothers prediction for condensation polymerization.
- ✔ **Crystallinity calculation:** $X_c = (\Delta H_f / \Delta H_f^0) \times 100\%$ — a KTU-favourite numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polysulphones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT751 - SPECIALITY POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polysulphones

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polysulphones (PSU)** are a distinguished class of **high-performance amorphous thermoplastic polymers** characterized by the presence of the aromatic **sulphone group (–SO₂–)** linked within their main polymer backbone. The sulfone moiety, flanked by phenylene rings, imparts exceptional thermal stability, oxidative resistance, and mechanical toughness, making them indispensable in engineering applications requiring service temperatures up to **180 °C** continuously.

The repeating unit of a generic aromatic polysulphone can be represented as:

$$\text{[–Ar–SO}_2\text{–Ar'–X–]}_n$$

where **Ar** and **Ar'** denote aromatic (phenylene) rings, and **X** represents an optional linking group such as **–O–** (ether), **–C(CH₃)₂–** (isopropylidene), or direct **–Ar–** (aryl) linkages.

### Major Commercial Polysulphone Families

| Trade Name | Polymer | Abbreviation | Tg (°C) |
|------------|---------|--------------|---------|
| Udel® | Polysulphone | PSU | ~190 |
| Victrex® PES | Polyethersulphone | PES | ~225 |
| Radel® R | Polyphenylsulphone | PPSU | ~220 |
| Ultrason® S | Polysulphone | PSU | ~190 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must distinguish between the four polymer families in the polysulphone class — Polysulphone (PSU), Polyethersulphone (PES), Polyphenylsulphone (PPSU), and Polyarylsulphone (PAS) — and recognize their unique repeating units.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Reinforced Concrete Bridge of Polymers"**
>
> Imagine constructing a bridge from two materials:
>
> 🔹 The **sulphone group (–SO₂–)** behaves like a **massive steel truss** embedded into the bridge deck. Its sulfur atom sits at the center of a tetrahedral arrangement, with two highly polarized, double-bonded oxygens gripping the polymer chain tightly. This creates **extraordinary bond rigidity**.
>
> 🔹 The **aromatic phenylene rings** act like **reinforced concrete slabs**, providing compressive strength and dimensional stability.
>
> 🔹 The **ether (–O–)** or **isopropylidene (–C(CH₃)₂–)** linkers function as **flexible expansion joints**, allowing just enough molecular motion to prevent brittleness without compromising rigidity.
>
> The combination of a **rigid load-bearing truss (SO₂)** + **strong slabs (Ar rings)** + **controlled flexibility (linkers)** yields a polymer that:
> - Does not soften easily under heat (high Tg)
> - Resists cracking under load (high toughness)
> - Refuses to burn (self-extinguishing)
> - Stays transparent (amorphous)
>
> This is precisely why polysulphones dominate **medical sterilization trays**, **aircraft interior panels**, and **hot-water plumbing fittings**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Engineering Standard Values):**
>
> - **Density:** **1.24 – 1.37 g/cm³**
> - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** **190 – 230 °C**
> - **Continuous Service Temperature:** **150 – 180 °C**
> - **Tensile Strength:** **70 – 90 MPa**
> - **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI):** **38 – 42 %** (inherently flame retardant)
> - **Refractive Index:** **1.63 – 1.65**
> - **Water Absorption (24 h):** **0.3 – 0.7 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the Sulfone Linkage

The defining feature of all polysulphones is the **–SO₂–** group. The sulfur atom exists in a **tetrahedral sp³ hybridization** with the geometry:

$$\text{S–O bond length} \approx 1.44 \text{ Å}$$
$$\text{O–S–O bond angle} \approx 119°}$$
$$\text{C–S–C bond angle} \approx 105°}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the –SO₂– group is the "backbone hero":**
>
> 1. The sulfur atom is in its **highest stable oxidation state (+6)**, making the polymer chemically inert toward oxidation.
> 2. The two **S=O double bonds** are highly polar (dipole moment ~4.5 D), generating strong **interchain dipole-dipole interactions** that elevate Tg.
> 3. The tetrahedral geometry creates a **sterically rigid kink**, disrupting chain packing enough to keep the polymer **amorphous and transparent**.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Polysulphones

### (A) Polysulphone (PSU) — Udel® Type

**Repeating unit:**

$$\text{–[O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{]–}_n$$

Contains three key units: **bisphenol-A ether**, **isopropylidene bridge**, and **diphenyl sulphone**.

### (B) Polyethersulphone (PES) — Victrex® Type

**Repeating unit:**

$$\text{–[O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–]–}_n$$

All aromatic, no aliphatic isopropylidene group → higher Tg (~225 °C), better oxidation resistance.

### (C) Polyphenylsulphone (PPSU) — Radel® R Type

**Repeating unit:**

$$\text{–[O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_3\text{–(biphenyl)–O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–]–}_n$$

Incorporates an additional **phenyl (biphenyl) linkage** → superior impact strength, hydrolysis resistance (passes 1000+ autoclave cycles).

### (D) Polyarylsulphone (PAS) — Astrel® Type

All-aryl backbone with no ether oxygen, providing the highest Tg (~275 °C) but extreme processing difficulty.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates all governing equations for polysulphone engineering. Memorize these for numerical and short-answer problems.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Glass Transition (Fox relation) | $\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ | Predicting Tg of copolymers/blends |
| 2 | Thermal Expansion (linear) | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | Dimensional design of molded parts |
| 3 | Modulus-Temperature (WLF) | $\log a_T = \frac{-C_1(T-T_r)}{C_2+(T-T_r)}$ | Viscoelastic flow prediction |
| 4 | Tensile Stress | $\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$ | Mechanical load capacity |
| 5 | Strain at Break | $\varepsilon_b = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Ductility assessment |
| 6 | Oxygen Permeability | $P = D \cdot S$ | Membrane separation design |
| 7 | Melt Viscosity (power law) | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Injection molding optimization |
| 8 | Thermal Conductivity | $q = -k \nabla T$ | Heat-exchanger service life |
| 9 | Limiting Oxygen Index | $\text{LOI} = \frac{[O_2]}{[O_2]+[N_2]} \times 100$ | Flame retardancy rating |
| 10 | Dielectric Loss Tangent | $\tan \delta = \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | High-frequency insulation |
| 11 | Number-Average Molar Mass | $\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Synthesis control |
| 12 | Weight-Average Molar Mass | $\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Mechanical property correlation |

> **Units:** Tg in **K**, α in **K⁻¹**, σ in **MPa**, ε dimensionless, LOI in **%**, η in **Pa·s**, tan δ dimensionless.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility Across Industries

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application Map of Polysulphones:**
>
> 🏥 **Medical:** Sterilizable surgical instrument trays, hemodialysis membranes, dental instruments (PPSU withstands 2000+ steam autoclave cycles).
>
> ✈️ **Aerospace:** Interior cabin panels, overhead luggage bins, seat frames (FAR 25.853 flame-smoke-toxicity compliance).
>
> 🚗 **Automotive:** Under-the-hood fuse housings, headlamp reflectors, supercharger impellers.
>
> 💧 **Membrane Technology:** Ultrafiltration (UF) and nanofiltration (NF) membranes for dairy, pharma, and water treatment.
>
> ⚡ **Electronics:** High-temperature PCB substrates, coil bobbins, fuse carriers.
>
> 🍽️ **Food Service:** Microwave-safe cookware, hot-water kettles, baby bottles (PPSU is BPA-free and dishwasher-safe).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Polysulphone Repeat Unit from Monomer Pair

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis Route 1 — Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution (NAS) for PES**

Consider the reaction between **bisphenol-S** (4,4′-dihydroxydiphenyl sulfone) and **4,4′-dichlorodiphenylsulfone** in the presence of an anhydrous base such as **K₂CO₃** and a polar aprotic solvent (**NMP** or **DMSO**).

**Step 1 — Phenoxide formation:**

The base deprotonates bisphenol-S to generate the **bisphenolate dianion** (nucleophile).

$$\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} + 2\,\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{KO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OK} + \text{KHCO}_3 + \text{K}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution (SNAr):**

The phenolate oxygen attacks the **ipso-carbon** bearing the chloride leaving group, forming a **Meisenheimer complex** (anionic intermediate stabilized by the para-SO₂ group), followed by elimination of KCl.

$$\text{ArO}^{-} + \text{Cl–Ar'–SO}_2\text{–Ar'–Cl} \longrightarrow [\text{ArO–Ar'–SO}_2\text{–Ar'–Cl}]^{-} \longrightarrow \text{ArO–Ar'–SO}_2\text{–Ar'–OAr} + 2\,\text{Cl}^{-}$$

**Step 3 — Step-Growth Propagation:**

Each condensation extends the chain by one repeat unit; the by-product is **KCl**, removed from the reaction mixture.

$$\begin{aligned}
n\,\text{HO–Ar–SO}_2\text{–Ar–OH} + n\,\text{Cl–Ar'–SO}_2\text{–Ar'–Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3,\,\text{NMP,}\,200°C} \\
\text{H–[O–Ar–SO}_2\text{–Ar–O–Ar'–SO}_2\text{–Ar']_n\text{–OH} + 2n\,\text{KCl}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Degree of Polymerization (Carothers equation for AA + BB type):**

For a stoichiometrically balanced step-growth polymerization:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

where **p** is the extent of reaction and **Xₙ** is the number-average degree of polymerization.

**Worked example:** To achieve a number-average molar mass $\bar{M}_n = 40{,}000$ g/mol for a PES repeat unit of M₀ = 232.3 g/mol:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} = \frac{40{,}000}{232.3} \approx 172.2$$

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p} \;\Longrightarrow\; p = 1 - \frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = 1 - \frac{1}{172.2} = 0.9942$$

Therefore, the reaction must proceed to **99.42 % completion** — a stringent requirement that explains why commercial PES synthesis uses high-purity monomers (>99.9 %) and extended reaction times.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Property Comparison of Polysulphone Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Property Matrix (engineering values, KTU exam-favorite)**

| Property | PSU (Udel®) | PES (Victrex®) | PPSU (Radel® R) | PAS (Astrel®) |
|----------|-------------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| Tg (°C) | 190 | 225 | 220 | 275 |
| HDT @ 1.82 MPa (°C) | 174 | 204 | 207 | 260 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 70 | 84 | 75 | 90 |
| Tensile Modulus (GPa) | 2.5 | 2.6 | 2.3 | 2.8 |
| Elongation at Break (%) | 60 | 40 | 60 | 15 |
| Notched Izod (J/m) | 70 | 85 | 700 | 70 |
| LOI (%) | 30 | 38 | 38 | 40 |
| Dielectric Strength (kV/mm) | 16 | 16 | 15 | 17 |
| Water Absorption 24 h (%) | 0.62 | 0.43 | 0.37 | 0.30 |
| Steam Sterilizable Cycles | ~100 | ~200 | >2000 | ~500 |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Medium | High | Very High |

> **Reading the Matrix:** Moving from PSU → PES → PPSU, the introduction of all-aromatic and biphenyl linkages boosts thermal and chemical resistance at the cost of processability (higher melt viscosity) and price.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — The Solubility Parameter Concept

For amorphous polymer solubility, the **Hildebrand solubility parameter** $\delta$ predicts compatibility:

$$\delta = \left(\frac{\Delta H_v - RT}{V_m}\right)^{1/2}$$

For polysulphones, the value is approximately **$\delta \approx 21.0 \text{ (J/cm}^3\text{)}^{1/2}$**, with hydrogen-bonding parameter $\delta_h \approx 7.5$.

This explains their **solubility only in polar aprotic solvents** (NMP, DMF, DMSO, CH₂Cl₂) and resistance to common organic solvents, fuels, and acids.

$$\Delta H_{\text{mix}} = V_{\text{mix}} \, \phi_1 \, \phi_2 \, (\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$$

A small $|\delta_1 - \delta_2|$ indicates mutual solubility; for most engineering solvents ($\delta \leq 19$), $|\Delta \delta| \geq 2$, making $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$ positive (non-favorable), confirming the chemical resistance of polysulphones.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Chemical Synthesis Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Monomers: Bisphenol-S] --> B[Step 1: Phenolate Formation with K2CO3]

    D --> E[Step 3: Meisenheimer Complex Intermediate]
    E --> F[Step 4: KCl Elimination]
    F --> G[Step 5: Step-Growth Propagation]
    G --> H[Step 6: Carothers DP Equation Applied]
    H --> I[Final Polyethersulphone PES Chain]
    I --> J[Pelletization and Drying]
    J --> K[Injection Molding or Extrusion]
    K --> L[Engineering Component: Membrane or Medical Tray]
```


## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Failure Mode and Mitigation Flow

```mermaid

    F1[Stress Concentration] --> F2[Crazing Initiation]

    F5 --> F7[Fillets and Radii Increase]

    F6 --> F9[Add 30 percent Glass Fiber]

    F8 --> F4
    F9 --> F4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Polysulphones with a suitable example. Highlight any two of their distinguishing properties. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Polysulphones are a class of engineering thermoplastics whose repeat units contain the aromatic **–SO₂– (sulphone) group** along with aromatic phenylene rings in the main chain. The most commercially important member is **polyethersulphone (PES)**, with the repeat unit:

$$\text{–[O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{]–}_n$$

Two distinguishing properties are:
1. **High glass transition temperature** (Tg = 190–225 °C), allowing continuous service up to 180 °C.
2. **Excellent hydrolytic and oxidative stability** due to the +6 oxidation state of the sulfur atom.

---

### Question 2: Why are polysulphones inherently flame retardant? Mention one quantitative metric. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The aromatic sulfone group decomposes endothermically and releases **SO₂ gas** upon heating, which dilutes flammable combustion gases and chokes the flame. The high aromatic content also promotes **charring**, forming a protective barrier. The quantitative metric is the **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)** of **38–42 %**, well above the threshold of 28 % for self-extinguishing classification.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat reaction scheme the **synthesis of polyethersulphone (PES)** by nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Discuss the role of the solvent and base used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **structure-property relationship** in polysulphones. How does the presence of the –SO₂– group influence the glass transition temperature and chemical resistance? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Synthesis of PES — Step-Growth via NAS:**

**Reaction scheme:**

$$\begin{aligned}
n\,\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} &+ n\,\text{Cl–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3,\,\text{NMP,}\,200°C} \\
&\text{H–[O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–SO}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{]}_n\text{–OH} + 2n\,\text{KCl}
\end{aligned}$$

**Role of solvent (NMP / DMSO):**
- Polar aprotic nature dissolves both monomers and growing polymer chain.
- High boiling point (~202 °C for NMP) allows reaction at temperatures that accelerate SNAr without premature precipitation (poor-solvent precipitation).
- Stabilizes the Meisenheimer intermediate by solvating the cation.

**Role of base (K₂CO₃):**
- Neutralizes HCl generated → forms KCl + KOH/KHCO₃, driving equilibrium forward.
- Generates the **phenolate nucleophile (ArO⁻)** from bisphenol-S, which is essential for SNAr.
- Mild base avoids side reactions (chain scission, ether cleavage) compared to NaOH.

**(b) Structure-Property Relationship:**

| Structural Feature | Property Consequence |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| Aromatic phenylene rings | Restrict rotation → **high Tg** |
| –SO₂– dipolar group | Strong interchain forces → **high Tg**, polar solubility |
| Ether (–O–) linkages | Provide segmental flexibility → **toughness, melt processability** |
| Fully aromatic backbone | **Chemical and oxidative resistance** |
| Tetrahedral –SO₂– geometry | Disrupts crystallization → **amorphous, transparent** |

The sulfur atom is in the **+6 oxidation state**, the highest stable form, making it chemically inert to oxidation. Combined with the electron-withdrawing nature of the sulphone group (which deactivates the aromatic ring towards electrophilic attack), this explains why polysulphones resist acids, bases, fuels, and hot water/steam.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the **structures, properties, and applications** of PSU, PES, and PPSU in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major applications** of polysulphones in (i) medical devices, (ii) aerospace, and (iii) membrane separation technology. Mention at least one specific grade used in each case. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparative Table:**

| Feature | PSU (Udel®) | PES (Victrex®) | PPSU (Radel® R) |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Repeat unit | Bisphenol-A + diphenyl sulfone | Bisphenol-S + dichlorodiphenyl sulfone | Bisphenol-S + biphenyl + dichlorodiphenyl sulfone |
| Tg (°C) | 190 | 225 | 220 |
| Steam cycles | ~100 | ~200 | >2000 |
| Impact strength | Moderate | Moderate-High | Very High (700 J/m) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical use | Plumbing fittings, food service | Membrane filters, aerospace | Reusable medical instruments |

**(b) Major Applications:**

**(i) Medical Devices:**
PPSU (Radel® R) is the gold standard for **reusable surgical instrument trays** and **sterilizable dental/orthopedic tools** because it withstands >2000 steam autoclave cycles (134 °C, 18 min) without yellowing, embrittlement, or losing mechanical strength.

**(ii) Aerospace:**
PSU and PES are used in **aircraft interior cabin components** — overhead bins, seat-back shells, air ducts — because they meet **FAR 25.853** flame-smoke-toxicity (FST) regulations with low heat release (<65 kW/m²) and minimal smoke emission.

**(iii) Membrane Separation:**
Asymmetric PES membranes (e.g., **PES MicroPES®** by 3M Membrana) dominate **ultrafiltration (UF)** for dairy whey protein concentration, **hemodialysis**, and **pharmaceutical cold sterilization**. Their controlled pore-size distribution (1–100 nm) and pH tolerance (1–13) make them ideal.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Polysulphone Questions:**
>
> ❌ **Trap 1:** Writing the sulphone group as **–SO₃–** (sulfonate) instead of **–SO₂–** (sulfone). These are entirely different chemistries; sulfonate is ionic.
>
> ❌ **Trap 2:** Confusing **PSU (Polysulphone)** with **PPSU (Polyphenylsulphone)**. The presence of the extra phenyl (biphenyl) linkage is the distinguishing structural feature.
>
> ❌ **Trap 3:** Stating that polysulphones are crystalline. They are **amorphous** — the tetrahedral kink of –SO₂– disrupts chain packing.
>
> ❌ **Trap 4:** Omitting units in numerical problems (Tg must be in **K** for the Fox equation, MPa for stress, % for LOI).
>
> ❌ **Trap 5:** Forgetting to mention **KCl elimination** as the by-product of the NAS synthesis of PES — this drives the equilibrium forward and is essential to the mechanism.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Polysulphones:**
>
> ✅ Polysulphones contain the **aromatic –SO₂– group** in the main chain.
>
> ✅ Four commercial families: **PSU, PES, PPSU, PAS** — distinguished by ether/isopropylidene/biphenyl content.
>
> ✅ **Tg range: 190–275 °C**, with PAS highest, PSU lowest.
>
> ✅ Sulfur is in the **+6 oxidation state** → oxidative inertness.
>
> ✅ **Synthesis:** Nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) for PES; Friedel-Crafts for PSU.
>
> ✅ **Carothers equation:** $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$; need ~99 % conversion for high molar mass.
>
> ✅ **Amorphous, transparent** (no crystallinity due to –SO₂– tetrahedral kink).
>
> ✅ **LOI = 30–42 %** → self-extinguishing, low smoke.
>
> ✅ **PPSU** withstands >2000 autoclave cycles → medical-grade applications.
>
> ✅ **PES membranes** dominate UF/DF in dairy and hemodialysis industries.
>
> ✅ **Solvent:** NMP/DMSO; **Base:** K₂CO₃ for PES synthesis.
>
> ✅ **ASTM standards** for testing: D638 (tensile), D790 (flexural), D648 (HDT), D2863 (LOI).
>
> ✅ Polysulphones are **engineering thermoplastics** — reprocessable, recyclable, but expensive.
>
> ✅ **Limitations:** Poor UV resistance (yellowing), attacked by polar aprotic solvents, notch-sensitive in thin sections.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyesters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT751 - SPECIALITY POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polyesters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polyesters** are a class of high-performance speciality polymers whose repeating structural units are joined together through **ester functional groups (–COO–)** formed by the condensation (step-growth) reaction between **dicarboxylic acids** (or their derivatives such as acid chlorides/anhydrides) and **dihydric alcohols (diols/glycols)**. The generic ester linkage is illustrated as:
> 
> $$-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{O}-$$

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT751)** classification under Module 1, polyesters are categorised as **linear, step-growth condensation polymers** with the general structural form:

$$\text{[–CO–R–COO–R'–O–]}_n$$

where **R** and **R'** are aliphatic or aromatic divalent organic residues. The **Carothers equation (1939)** governs their molecular weight build-up:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

where *p* is the extent of reaction and $\overline{X}_n$ is the number-average degree of polymerisation. A high molecular weight polyester therefore demands **p ≥ 0.99**, which is industrially achieved by precisely stoichiometric monomer balance and continuous removal of the condensate (water/methanol/HCl).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Train Coupling of Organic Chemistry":**
> 
> Imagine a long train where each carriage is a small organic molecule. To join two carriages, you need a "coupling mechanism" — in polyesters, that mechanism is the **ester linkage (–COO–)**. A **dicarboxylic acid** is like a carriage with **two hooks** (–COOH groups) on either end, and a **diol** is a carriage with **two loops** (–OH groups). When a hook meets a loop, they snap together and release a **water molecule** (the "exhaust steam" of the coupling). Linking thousands of such carriages produces the polymer chain.
> 
> The "speciality" arises when the carriages are made of *aromatic rings* (rigid, like steel carriages) or *fluorinated units* (slippery carriages) — these impart high strength, thermal resistance, or chemical inertness to the final polymer.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Class | Representative Polymer | Key Trait |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated Aliphatic | **PCL, PEA** | Biodegradable, flexible |
| Saturated Aromatic | **PET, PBT, PEN** | High strength, barrier properties |
| Unsaturated | **UPE (with styrene)** | Thermoset, fibre-reinforced |
| Liquid Crystalline | **Vectra (HBA/HNA)** | Self-reinforcing |
| Engineering | **Polycarbonate (PC)** | Tough, transparent |
| Bio-based | **PLA, PHB-class** | Renewable, compostable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 1 places strong emphasis on **synthesis routes, structure–property relationships, and end-use performance** of polyesters, especially **PET, PBT, PC, and unsaturated polyesters**, which are the most frequently tested in ESE.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Ester bond energy:** ≈ **350 kJ/mol**
- **Tg of PET:** ≈ **75–80 °C**
- **Tm of PET:** ≈ **260–265 °C**
- **Repeat unit molecular weight of PET:** = **192 g/mol**
- **Density of amorphous PET:** ≈ **1.33 g/cm³**; crystalline PET ≈ **1.45 g/cm³**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-Growth Polymerisation Theory (Carothers Framework)

The formation of polyesters is a classic example of **step-growth (condensation) polymerisation** discovered by **Wallace Carothers** in the 1930s at DuPont. The mechanism proceeds in **discrete, independent steps**, where any two reactive species (monomer, oligomer, or polymer) can react at any time.

### 2.1.1 Key Logical Steps of Step-Growth

1. **Functional Group Encounter** — A –COOH (or –COCl) group of one molecule collides with an –OH group of another.
2. **Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution** — The lone pair on the oxygen of –OH attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
3. **Tetrahedral Intermediate** — A short-lived four-coordinate intermediate forms.
4. **Elimination of Condensate** — A small molecule (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH, or phenol) is expelled, completing the ester bond.
5. **Chain Growth by Equal Reactivity** — Critically, all –COOH and –OH groups are assumed **equally reactive**, regardless of chain length. Hence molecular weight increases **slowly** until high conversion is reached.
6. **Stoichiometric Control** — Exact 1:1 molar balance of acid:hydroxyl is essential; any imbalance (denoted *r*) caps chain ends and limits $\overline{X}_n$.

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why p must approach 1:** Since $\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$, when *r* = 1 (perfect stoichiometry), $\overline{X}_n$ tends to infinity only as *p* → 1.
- **How industry achieves high *p*:** Use of **excess volatile diol** removed late in the process (e.g., ethylene glycol in PET), **high vacuum polycondensation**, and **solid-state polymerisation (SSP)** for the final molecular weight boost.
- **Why esterification is equilibrium-limited:** Water must be continuously removed; otherwise, the reverse hydrolysis reaction "unzips" the polymer.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number-average DP (equal stoich) | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | p = extent of reaction | Dimensionless |
| 2 | DP with stoichiometric imbalance | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | r = N$_{\text{OH}}$/N$_{\text{COOH}}$ ≤ 1 | r = 1 is ideal |
| 3 | Weight-average DP | $\overline{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | For Flory distribution | When r = 1 |
| 4 | Polydispersity Index | PDI = $\dfrac{\overline{X}_w}{\overline{X}_n} = 1 + p$ | Approaches 2 at p→1 | Step-growth signature |
| 5 | Repeat unit of PET | $\text{M}_0 = \text{M}_{\text{TPA}} + \text{M}_{\text{EG}} - 2\text{M}_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ | 166 + 62 − 36 = 192 | g/mol |
| 6 | Intrinsic viscosity (Mark–Houwink) | $[\eta] = K\,\overline{M}_v^{a}$ | K, a are polymer–solvent constants | dL/g |
| 7 | Flory–Huggins interaction | $\Delta G_m = RT[\dfrac{\phi_1}{x_1}\ln\phi_1 + \phi_2\ln\phi_2 + \chi\phi_1\phi_2]$ | $\chi$ = interaction parameter | Solubility criterion |
| 8 | Glass transition (Fox equation) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | For copolymer blends | K |
| 9 | Avrami crystallisation | $1 - X_c = \exp(-kt^n)$ | n = Avrami index | For PET cold crystallisation |
| 10 | Esterification rate (acid-catalysed) | $-\dfrac{d[\text{COOH}]}{dt} = k[\text{COOH}][\text{OH}]$ | Second order overall | mol L$^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** In KTU ESE, marks for a 14-mark question are commonly lost when students forget the **condensate term (–H₂O or –HCl)** in calculating the repeat-unit molecular weight. Always subtract **2 × M$_{\text{condensate}}$** for each ester linkage formed.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Polyesters

- **Packaging:** PET bottles (carbonated beverages) — 70 % of global PET goes into bottles.
- **Textiles:** PET fibres as "polyester fabric" — wrinkle resistance, quick drying.
- **Electronics:** PET film as dielectric in capacitors, flexible printed circuits.
- **Automotive:** PBT for connectors, housings, and under-bonnet parts (Tg ≈ 50 °C, high heat-deflection).
- **Construction:** Unsaturated polyester resins (UPR) reinforced with glass fibre for **FRP tanks, pipes, boat hulls**.
- **Medical:** Bioresorbable polyesters (PLA, PGA, PCL) for **sutures, scaffolds, drug-delivery** devices.
- **3-D Printing:** PLA filament dominates the FDM desktop-printer market.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Carothers Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation

### Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction *p*

> Let *N*$_0$ be the total number of functional groups (–COOH or –OH) initially present in the reaction mixture. At time *t*, the number of unreacted groups is *N*. The **extent of reaction** is defined as the fraction of functional groups that have reacted.

$$
p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the unreacted fraction

Rearranging Step 1:

$$
N = N_0\,(1 - p)
$$

### Step 3 — Count the number of molecules remaining

> In step-growth polymerisation, the formation of each new ester bond **joins two molecules into one**, thereby reducing the total number of molecules by one. Let the total number of monomer units (repeat-unit equivalents) originally present be *N*$_0$/2 for a difunctional system (since each monomer has two functional groups). The number of *molecules* present at conversion *p* is therefore:

$$
N_{\text{mol}} = \dfrac{N_0}{2} - (\text{number of bonds formed}) = \dfrac{N_0}{2} - \dfrac{N_0\,p}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{X}_n$

> The degree of polymerisation is the **average number of repeat units per polymer molecule**, equal to the original number of monomer units divided by the molecules remaining:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{Initial number of monomer molecules}}{\text{Number of molecules at time }t}
$$

Substituting the result of Step 3:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{N_0/2}{N_0(1-p)/2} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

- As *p* → 1 (complete reaction), $\overline{X}_n$ → ∞. **This is the "step-growth curse":** you must reach >99 % conversion to obtain commercially useful molecular weights.
- At *p* = 0.98, $\overline{X}_n$ = **50** only — too low for fibre spinning.
- At *p* = 0.995, $\overline{X}_n$ = **200** — typical for film-grade PET.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Carothers Equation with Stoichiometric Imbalance

### Step 1 — Define the stoichiometric ratio *r*

> Let the reaction mixture contain *N_A* acid groups and *N_B* hydroxyl groups, with *N_A* ≤ *N_B*. The **stoichiometric imbalance ratio** is:

$$
r = \dfrac{N_A}{N_B}, \quad 0 < r \leq 1
$$

The **minority species** (acid, with *N_A* groups) will eventually be completely consumed. The number of bonds formed equals *N_A* × *p* (where *p* is the fraction of *minority* groups reacted).

### Step 2 — Count molecules remaining

Initial monomer count: each molecule contributes two groups, so the total number of monomer *molecules* initially is:

$$
N_{\text{initial}} = \dfrac{N_A + N_B}{2}
$$

After reaction, unreacted groups = *N_A*(1 − *p*) + *N_B* − *N_A*·*p* = *N_B* − *N_A*·*p* (note: *N_A*·*p* acid groups have reacted, and an equal number of hydroxyl groups have been consumed).

The number of bonds formed = *N_A*·*p*. Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{final}} = \dfrac{N_A + N_B}{2} - N_A\,p
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $\overline{X}_n$

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{(N_A + N_B)/2}{(N_A + N_B)/2 - N_A\,p} = \dfrac{(1 + r)/2}{(1 + r)/2 - r\,p} = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2\,r\,p}
$$

This is the **generalised Carothers equation**.

### Step 4 — Limiting case check

Setting *r* = 1:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{2}{2 - 2p} = \dfrac{1}{1-p} \quad \checkmark
$$

The derivation correctly collapses to the equal-stoichiometry result.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Repeat-Unit Molecular Weight of PET

### Step 1 — Identify monomers

- **Terephthalic acid (TPA):** C$_6$H$_4$(COOH)$_2$, M = 166.13 g/mol
- **Ethylene glycol (EG):** HOCH$_2$CH$_2$OH, M = 62.07 g/mol

### Step 2 — Write the condensation reaction

$$
\text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COOH} + \text{HO-CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{-OH} \longrightarrow \text{[OC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COO-CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{-O]}_n + 2n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the repeat unit mass

For **one** repeat unit of PET, one TPA molecule and one EG molecule combine, releasing **two water molecules** (one from each end of the TPA):

$$
M_0 = M_{\text{TPA}} + M_{\text{EG}} - 2\,M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

$$
M_0 = 166.13 + 62.07 - 2 \times 18.02
$$

$$
M_0 = 228.20 - 36.04 = 192.16 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate the degree of polymerisation for a target molecular weight

For commercial bottle-grade PET, $\overline{M}_n$ ≈ 30 000 g/mol:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \dfrac{30\,000}{192.16} \approx 156
$$

Implies *p* ≈ 0.9936.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — Number-Average Molecular Weight in Imbalanced Systems

Using the generalised Carothers equation together with the empirical relation $\overline{M}_n = M_0\,\overline{X}_n + M_{\text{end group}}$ (often approximated as $\overline{M}_n \approx M_0\,\overline{X}_n$ for high *p*):

$$
\overline{M}_n = M_0 \times \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}
$$

For **PET** synthesised with a 2 mol% excess of ethylene glycol (*r* = 1/1.02 ≈ 0.980) at *p* = 0.99:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1.980}{1.980 - 2 \times 0.980 \times 0.99} = \dfrac{1.980}{1.980 - 1.9404} = \dfrac{1.980}{0.0396} = 50
$$

The **practical lesson** is that even a 2 % imbalance at *p* = 0.99 caps the chain length at ~50 units — disastrous for fibre-grade polymer. **Stoichiometric control is non-negotiable.**

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of Common Polyesters

| Property | PET | PBT | PEN | PC | PLA | UPE (cured) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T$_g$ (°C) | **78** | 50 | 120 | **150** | 60 | ~120 (post-cure) |
| T$_m$ (°C) | **260** | 225 | 270 | amorphous | 175 | thermoset |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 75 | 55 | 90 | 65 | 60 | 50–80 |
| Modulus (GPa) | 2.8 | 2.5 | 3.5 | 2.4 | 3.5 | 3.0–4.0 |
| Elongation at break (%) | 30 | 100 | 25 | 110 | 6 | 2–3 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.38 | 1.31 | 1.36 | 1.20 | 1.24 | 1.20–1.30 |
| Moisture uptake (%) | 0.1 | 0.08 | 0.1 | 0.2 | 0.5 | 0.2 |
| O$_2$ permeability (cc·mil/100in²·day) | 6 | 10 | 3 | 300 | 30 | – |
| Biodegradable? | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| Typical application | Bottles | Connectors | Hot-fill | Glazing | 3D printing | FRP composites |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of PET Synthesis & Microstructure (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MonomerA["Monomer A: Terephthalic Acid HOOC-C6H4-COOH"] --> R1
    MonomerB["Monomer B: Ethylene Glycol HO-CH2CH2-OH"] --> R1
    R1{"Esterification / Transesterification Stage 1: 250-280 C, Catalyst ZnO / Sb2O3"} --> C1
    C1["Condensate: H2O or CH3OH removed by distillation column"] --> V1
    C1 --> P1
    P1["BHET: Bis 2-Hydroxyethyl Terephthalate oligomers, DP approx 5-10"] --> R2
    R2{"Polycondensation Stage 2: 280-290 C, High Vacuum less than 1 mbar, Sb2O3 Catalyst"} --> P2
    P2["Prepolymer melt DP approx 30-50"] --> R3
    R3{"Solid State Polymerisation SSP: 200-230 C, N2 atmosphere, 10-30 hours"} --> P3
    P3["Final PET pellet DP approx 100-150, IV approx 0.7-0.8 dL per g"] --> Q
    V1["Volatiles recycled to glycol recovery"] --> X["Waste handling"]

    style R1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF

    style P3 fill:#32CD32,color:#FFF

```

## 4.2 Architecture of a Saturated Aromatic Polyester Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Di-acid segment: -OC-Ar-CO-"] --- B["Di-ol segment: -O-CH2-CH2-O-"]
    B --- C["Di-acid segment: -OC-Ar-CO-"]
    C --- D["Di-ol segment: -O-CH2-CH2-O-"]
    D --- E["Di-acid segment: -OC-Ar-CO-"]
    E --- F["Di-ol segment: -O-CH2-CH2-O-"]
    F -.-> G["... n times ..."]
    G --- H["Terminal -COOH or -OH end cap"]
    H --- I["Terminal -OH or -COOH end cap"]

    style A fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000
    style B fill:#ADD8E6,color:#000
    style D fill:#ADD8E6,color:#000
    style F fill:#ADD8E6,color:#000
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style H fill:#F0E68C,color:#000
    style I fill:#F0E68C,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Decision Flow for Polyester Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Engineering design requirement"] --> Q1{"Operating temperature above 150 C?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> P1["Use PEN, PET, or High-Tg PC"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Need transparency and impact resistance?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> P2["Use Polycarbonate PC"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Need chemical resistance and barrier?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> P3["Use PET or PBT"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Need biodegradability or compostability?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> P4["Use PLA, PHB, or PCL"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> Q5{"Need thermoset and fibre reinforcement?"}
    Q5 -- "Yes" --> P5["Use Unsaturated Polyester Resin UPR with styrene"]
    Q5 -- "No" --> P6["Re-evaluate design requirements"]

    style Start fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000

    style P5 fill:#F08080,color:#FFF
    style P6 fill:#D3D3D3,color:#000

    style Q2 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000
```
