```mermaid
    style Q4 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000
    style Q5 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Esterification Reaction Mechanism (Block-Level Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Protonation of carbonyl oxygen of carboxylic acid by acid catalyst H plus"] --> S2
    S2["Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of alcohol oxygen on activated carbonyl carbon"] --> S3
    S3["Step 3: Tetrahedral intermediate: positive charge on alcohol oxygen, four substituents on carbon"] --> S4
    S4["Step 4: Proton transfer from alcohol O to one of the original OH groups on former acid"] --> S5
    S5["Step 5: Loss of water molecule H2O from tetrahedral intermediate: leaves as protonated water"] --> S6
    S6["Step 6: Deprotonation of carbonyl oxygen regenerates acid catalyst and yields the neutral ester C-O-C=O bond"] --> P["Product: Stable ester linkage within polyester chain"]

    style S1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style S6 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style P fill:#32CD32,color:#FFF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define polyesters. Give two examples with their applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Polyesters are step-growth condensation polymers whose **repeating units are linked by ester groups (–COO–)** formed between dicarboxylic acids (or their derivatives) and diols.
> 
> **Examples:**
> 1. **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** — used in **beverage bottles and textile fibres** because of its high strength, clarity, and barrier properties.
> 2. **PC (Polycarbonate of Bisphenol A)** — used in **safety goggles, CDs, and glazing panels** due to its high impact strength and optical transparency.
> 
> *(1 mark definition + 1 mark example 1 + 1 mark example 2)*

### Q2. What is the significance of stoichiometric balance in step-growth polymerisation of polyesters?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Carothers equation $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ shows that the **degree of polymerisation is highly sensitive to the stoichiometric ratio *r***. When *r* < 1 (i.e., one functional group is in slight excess), the chain ends are **capped** by the excess reactant, halting further growth. For example, a 1 % molar excess of diol in PET synthesis at *p* = 0.99 limits $\overline{X}_n$ to about 100, whereas perfect stoichiometry yields $\overline{X}_n$ = 100 (in this case numerically similar, but the divergence widens dramatically at higher *p*). Hence **rigorous stoichiometric control is essential** to obtain high-molecular-weight polyesters suitable for fibre or film applications.
> 
> *(1 mark Carothers reference + 1 mark effect of imbalance + 1 mark industrial implication)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Polyester Synthesis & Kinetics

**(a)** Describe the **two-stage industrial synthesis of PET** from terephthalic acid and ethylene glycol. Mention the role of catalysts, the by-products removed at each stage, and the conditions of temperature, pressure, and time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Carothers equation** for a non-stoichiometric mixture of difunctional monomers. A PET polymerisation is carried out with an initial acid-to-hydroxyl ratio *r* = 0.985. If the extent of reaction reaches *p* = 0.99, calculate $\overline{X}_n$ and $\overline{M}_n$. Comment on whether this polymer is suitable for bottle-grade applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

> **Stage 1 — Esterification (Direct Esterification, 250–280 °C, 4–6 bar, 2–4 h):**
> Terephthalic acid (TPA) reacts with ethylene glycol (EG) in a molar ratio of **1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.2** (slight excess of glycol to drive equilibrium). **No external catalyst** is needed for direct esterification because TPA is itself a weak acid that catalyses the reaction. The product is a mixture of **BHET (bis-2-hydroxyethyl terephthalate) and low oligomers** (DP ≈ 5–10).
> 
> $$\text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COOH} + 2\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{-OOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COO-CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
> 
> The by-product **water** is removed continuously via a reflux/distillation column.
> 
> **Stage 2 — Polycondensation (280–290 °C, < 1 mbar, 4–6 h):**
> The BHET oligomers undergo further transesterification/condensation in the **melt** under **high vacuum** to remove excess EG and push *p* toward 0.99. **Antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃, 200–400 ppm)** is added as the polycondensation catalyst. T$_m$ rises to ~260 °C at DP ≈ 100.
> 
> **Optional Stage 3 — Solid-State Polymerisation (SSP) (200–230 °C, N₂, 10–30 h):**
> Amorphous pellets are crystallised and then heated below T$_m$ in inert gas to raise IV from ~0.6 to 0.8–1.0 dL/g (food-grade bottles).
> 
> *Mark split: 2 marks stage 1, 3 marks stage 2, 1 mark SSP, 1 mark catalyst/by-product summary.*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

> **Derivation:** (follow § 3.2 above step by step)
> 
> Step 1: *r* = N$_A$/N$_B$ = 0.985, *p* = 0.99.
> 
> Step 2: substitute into generalised Carothers equation:
> 
> $$
> \overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp} = \dfrac{1.985}{1.985 - 2 \times 0.985 \times 0.99}
> $$
> 
> Step 3: evaluate the denominator:
> 
> $$
> 2 \times 0.985 \times 0.99 = 1.9503
> $$
> 
> $$
> 1.985 - 1.9503 = 0.0347
> $$
> 
> Step 4: compute $\overline{X}_n$:
> 
> $$
> \overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1.985}{0.0347} \approx 57.2
> $$
> 
> Step 5: compute $\overline{M}_n$ using M$_0$ = 192 g/mol:
> 
> $$
> \overline{M}_n = 192 \times 57.2 \approx 11\,000\;\text{g/mol}
> $$
> 
> **Comment:** This $\overline{M}_n$ is **far too low** for bottle-grade PET (which requires $\overline{M}_n$ ≈ 30 000 g/mol and IV ≈ 0.7–0.8 dL/g). The imbalance of 1.5 % has catastrophically limited the chain length. To achieve bottle-grade polymer, *r* must be ≥ 0.998 and *p* must reach 0.995+.
> 
> *Mark split: 2 marks derivation, 3 marks numerical substitution, 2 marks comment on suitability.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Structure–Property Relationships

**(a)** Explain the **structure–property relationship** in saturated aromatic polyesters with specific reference to **PET, PBT, and PEN**. Discuss how the para- vs meta- arrangement of the aromatic ring influences T$_g$, T$_m$, and barrier properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on **(i) Unsaturated Polyester Resins (UPR)** and **(ii) Liquid-Crystalline Polyesters (LCP)**. Compare their processing methods and end-use applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

> All three polymers are built from the **ethylene glycol unit (–OCH₂CH₂O–)** combined with an aromatic diacid:
> 
> | Polymer | Aromatic Diacid | Linkage Geometry |
> |---|---|---|
> | PET | Terephthalic acid (1,4-benzene) | **Para** |
> | PBT | Terephthalic acid + 1,4-butane diol | **Para** (with longer diol) |
> | PEN | 2,6-Naphthalene dicarboxylate | **Para-naphthyl** (extended aromatic) |
> 
> **PET (para-phenylene):** Rigid, linear chain, efficient chain packing. **T$_g$ ≈ 78 °C, T$_m$ ≈ 260 °C.** Excellent gas barrier. Used in bottles, films, fibres.
> 
> **PBT (para-phenylene + butylene diol):** The longer –(CH₂)₄– segment introduces a **"kink"** that disrupts crystallinity, lowering T$_m$ to 225 °C. The polymer crystallises **faster** than PET (shorter mould-cycle in injection moulding) and has higher impact strength. Used in automotive connectors.
> 
> **PEN (2,6-naphthalene):** The **naphthalene unit** is longer and more rigid than benzene, giving **T$_g$ ≈ 120 °C, T$_m$ ≈ 270 °C.** Oxygen permeability is **3–5× lower** than PET, UV barrier is superior. Used in hot-fill bottles, advanced capacitor films.
> 
> **Meta- vs Para-effect:** Meta-substituted aromatic diacids (e.g., isophthalic acid) introduce a **120° kink** that prevents crystallisation, yielding **amorphous, transparent** polyesters. They have lower T$_g$ and T$_m$, higher solubility, and are used in coatings and hot-melt adhesives.
> 
> *Mark split: 2 marks structural diagrams/identification, 3 marks property discussion, 2 marks meta- vs para-comparison.*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(i)+(ii)

> **(i) Unsaturated Polyester Resin (UPR):**
> Synthesised by reacting **saturated diacids (e.g., phthalic anhydride)** with **unsaturated diacids (e.g., maleic anhydride)** and a diol (propylene glycol). The product is a **viscous, linear oligomer** with C=C double bonds along the backbone. It is **dissolved in styrene monomer (30–40 %)** to reduce viscosity.
> 
> **Curing:** Initiated by a peroxide catalyst (e.g., MEKP) at room temperature. The styrene acts as a **cross-linker**, copolymerising with the C=C sites to form a **3-D thermoset network**. Heat resistance and mechanical strength are excellent after cure.
> 
> **Applications:** Glass-fibre-reinforced plastics (GRP) for boat hulls, chemical tanks, shower stalls, automotive body panels.
> 
> **(ii) Liquid-Crystalline Polyesters (LCP):**
> Built from **rigid, rod-like monomers** (e.g., 4-hydroxybenzoic acid HBA + 6-hydroxy-2-naphthoic acid HNA, trade name **Vectra**). The chains align spontaneously in the melt, forming **domains of nematic order** that act as "molecular rebar".
> 
> **Processing:** Injection moulding, extrusion. Very **low melt viscosity** (aligned chains slide past each other), **near-zero mould shrinkage**, and exceptional **dimensional stability**.
> 
> **Applications:** High-precision connectors, surface-mount device (SMD) housings, microwave cookware, fibre optics.
> 
> **Comparison:**
> - UPR is a **thermoset** requiring curing; LCP is a **thermotropic thermoplastic** that re-melts reversibly.
> - UPR is **cheaper** and used in large structures; LCP is **expensive** and used in miniaturised electronics.
> - Both have **excellent chemical resistance and mechanical performance.**
> 
> *Mark split: 3 marks UPR, 3 marks LCP, 1 mark comparison.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the –2 H₂O in repeat-unit mass calculation** for PET, PEN, PBT — a 36 g/mol error that propagates into $\overline{X}_n$.
> 2. **Confusing *r* = N$_A$/N$_B$ with N$_B$/N$_A$** — the minority group must always be in the numerator (≤ 1).
> 3. **Omitting the catalyst name and concentration** in synthesis questions — examiners specifically allocate marks for Sb₂O₃, ZnO, Ti(OBu)₄.
> 4. **Treating PET as a thermoplastic with a "curing" step** — PET is **not** a thermoset; it does **not** undergo cross-linking in the bottle moulding step.
> 5. **Ignoring the directionality of the ester bond** — the –COO– group is **not** the same as –OOC–; structure drawing marks require the carbonyl on the correct side.
> 6. **Writing "polyester resin" instead of "unsaturated polyester resin"** for UPR — clarity of terminology matters for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Polyesters = step-growth polymers** with –COO– linkages; synthesised by condensation of –COOH and –OH groups.
- [x] **Carothers equation** $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ — governs molecular weight; **r = 1 is ideal**.
- [x] **PDI of step-growth ≈ 2** at high conversion — a defining signature vs. chain-growth (PDI ≈ 1).
- [x] **PET repeat unit M₀ = 192 g/mol** = 166 (TPA) + 62 (EG) − 36 (2 H₂O).
- [x] **PET T$_g$ ≈ 78 °C, T$_m$ ≈ 260 °C**; bottle grade requires $\overline{M}_n$ ≈ 30 000 g/mol.
- [x] **PBT** has faster crystallisation, lower T$_m$ (225 °C), suited to injection moulding of connectors.
- [x] **PEN** has superior thermal and barrier properties due to the **rigid naphthalene ring**.
- [x] **Para-substitution → crystallinity; meta-substitution → amorphous transparency**.
- [x] **UPR** is a **thermoset**, cured with styrene and a peroxide initiator; used in FRP composites.
- [x] **LCP (e.g., Vectra)** contains rigid-rod monomers; self-reinforcing, low-shrinkage moulding, used in electronics.
- [x] **Polycarbonate (PC of BPA)** is a polyester with a carbonate ester; T$_g$ ≈ 150 °C, transparent, high impact.
- [x] **Biodegradable polyesters** (PLA, PGA, PCL) used in medical implants; degrade by ester hydrolysis.
- [x] **Industrial PET** is made in **2 stages**: esterification (250–280 °C, autogenous pressure) → polycondensation (280–290 °C, high vacuum, Sb₂O₃) → optional **SSP** for IV boost.
- [x] **Equilibrium removal of condensate (H₂O, CH₃OH, HCl)** is essential — Le Chatelier's principle in action.
- [x] **End-group analysis** (titration of –COOH or –OH) and **intrinsic-viscosity measurement** are the two standard industrial QC methods for polyester molecular weight.
- [x] **Transesterification** is the key side reaction that allows PET recycling via methanolysis, glycolysis, or hydrolysis back to monomers.
- [x] **Crystallinity of PET** can be quantified by **Avrami equation** $1 - X_c = e^{-kt^n}$; n ≈ 2–3 for spherulitic growth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aromatic Polyamides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT751 - SPECIALITY POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aromatic Polyamides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aromatic Polyamides** (commonly abbreviated as **Aramids** — *Aromatic Polyamides*) are a distinguished class of high-performance synthetic polymers in which the recurring amide linkage ($-\text{CO}-\text{NH}-$) is directly bonded to at least **85% of the aromatic ring carbons** (as defined by the U.S. Federal Trade Commission, FTC). When both the diamine and the diacid components carry aromatic (benzene) rings in the polymer backbone, the resulting macromolecule exhibits a unique combination of extraordinary thermal stability, mechanical strength, flame resistance, and chemical inertness.

The two commercially dominant members of this family are:
- **Nomex** (Poly-*meta*-phenylene isophthalamide, abbreviated **MPIA** or **m-aramid**)
- **Kevlar** (Poly-*para*-phenylene terephthalamide, abbreviated **PPTA** or **p-aramid**)

The general repeating unit of an aramid may be represented schematically as:

$$
\left[ -\text{CO}-\text{Ar}_1-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{Ar}_2-\text{NH}-\text{Ar}_3-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{Ar}_4- \right]_n
$$

where $\text{Ar}_1, \text{Ar}_2, \text{Ar}_3, \text{Ar}_4$ denote aromatic rings whose ring-substitution pattern (*ortho*, *meta*, or *para*) governs the polymer's crystallinity, chain rigidity, and end-use performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEPOT751 / Module 1**
> Aromatic polyamides form the cornerstone of the "High-Performance Fibres & Engineering Thermoplastics" cluster. Students are expected to clearly distinguish between *meta*- and *para*-linked aramids, articulate the structure–property relationships that govern their superior behaviour, and write balanced equations for their synthesis via interfacial and low-temperature solution polycondensation routes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel-Reinforced Nylon Ladder"

To a student encountering aramids for the very first time, the molecule can be visualised as a **ladder made of alternating benzene "rungs" connected by rigid amide "side-pieces"**, where each amide group is locked against its neighbour by **hydrogen bonds acting like ratchet straps** between adjacent ladder rails.

| Familiar Everyday Object | Analogy in Aromatic Polyamide |
|---|---|
| **Nylon 6,6 rope** (regular aliphatic polyamide) | A flexible cotton rope — strong, but stretches and melts at ~260 °C |
| **Steel cable** (Kevlar-49 tow) | A rigid steel wire — 5× stronger per weight, survives 500 °C short-term |
| **Wool sweater** (hydrogen-bonded natural fibre) | A fabric held together by reversible H-bonds; aramid's H-bonds are unusually *strong* and *dense* |

**Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine taking ordinary Nylon 6,6 and replacing every flexible $-\text{(CH}_2)_6-$ segment with a **flat benzene ring**. The polymer chain can no longer coil, fold, or rotate freely. It locks into a near-linear, rod-like conformation. When you then align millions of these rod-like chains parallel to one another and tie them together with the strong $\text{N-H}\cdots\text{O=C}$ hydrogen bonds, the resulting fibre behaves more like a **metallic wire drawn from a polymer melt** than like a conventional textile fibre. That, in essence, is the magic of an aramid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Hydrogen Bonding in Aramids**
> In an aramid, every amide proton ($\text{N-H}$) on one chain points directly at the lone-pair-bearing carbonyl oxygen ($\text{C=O}$) of a neighbouring chain. Because the amide groups are *para*-substituted on a rigid ring, the chains pack into an **almost crystalline sheet**, with two-dimensional H-bond networks running perpendicular to the chain axis. This is fundamentally different from aliphatic nylons, where H-bonds are spaced further apart and chains can rotate.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Performance Metrics

| Metric (Symbol) | Kevlar 49 (PPTA) | Nomex 410 (MPIA) | Aliphatic Nylon 6,6 (Reference) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glass-transition temperature ($T_g$) | None (decomposes) | ~275 °C | ~50 °C |
| Melting point ($T_m$) | None (decomposes >500 °C) | None (decomposes ~370 °C) | ~265 °C |
| **Tensile strength** | **3.6 GPa** | **0.5 GPa** | 0.08 GPa |
| **Young's modulus** | **130 GPa** | 17 GPa | 2.8 GPa |
| Density ($\rho$) | **1.44 g·cm⁻³** | 1.38 g·cm⁻³ | 1.14 g·cm⁻³ |
| Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI) | ~29 % | **~28–30 %** | ~20–22 % |

These figures are critical for any KTU numerical/comparative question and must be committed to memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Classification — Meta vs Para Substitution

The single most decisive structural variable in an aramid is the **ring-substitution geometry** of the aromatic diamine and diacid moieties. This dictates chain linearity, packing density, and consequently mechanical/thermal performance.

### 2.1.1 *Para*-Aramids (e.g., Kevlar / PPTA)

In PPTA, both the *p*-phenylenediamine (PPD) and terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) rings are substituted **1,4** (i.e., diametrically across the ring). The chain therefore propagates in a strictly **linear (zig-zag) fashion**:

$$
\text{H}_2\text{N}-\!\!\underset{\text{1,4-C}_6\text{H}_4}{\boxed{}}\!\!-\text{NH}_2 \;+\; \text{ClOC}-\!\!\underset{\text{1,4-C}_6\text{H}_4}{\boxed{}}\!\!-\text{COCl}
$$

Because each amide linkage is *para*, the amide bond vector and the ring axis are **collinear**. The result is a **rigid-rod polymer** that:

- Cannot fold into crystalline lamellae (no chain folding → extended-chain crystal).
- Forms **liquid-crystalline phases** in concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (negative dielectric anisotropy drives spontaneous chain alignment).
- Develops exceptionally high **axial modulus** upon spinning.

### 2.1.2 *Meta*-Aramids (e.g., Nomex / MPIA)

In MPIA, the *m*-phenylenediamine (MPD) is substituted **1,3**, and the isophthaloyl chloride (IPC) is also **1,3**. The chain therefore propagates with an inherent **120° kink** at each ring. The polymer is **semi-flexible** rather than truly rod-like.

The consequence: lower crystallinity, lower modulus, but **higher ductility, better dyeability, and superior dielectric breakdown strength** — qualities that make Nomex ideal for **electrical insulation paper and fire-resistant garments**.

---

## 2.2 Role of Hydrogen Bonding

Every amide group is both a proton donor ($\text{N-H}$) and acceptor ($\text{C=O}$). The hydrogen-bond energy in aramids is approximately **20–40 kJ·mol⁻¹**, which is far stronger than in aliphatic polyamides (typically 8–12 kJ·mol⁻¹) because:

1. The amide groups are **conjugated** with the aromatic ring (resonance), increasing the partial positive charge on $\text{H}$ and partial negative charge on $\text{O}$.
2. The rigid *para*-geometry forces all amide groups to participate in **two-dimensional H-bonded sheets** rather than disperse randomly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of H-Bonded Sheets**
> The H-bonded sheet structure is what enables Kevlar fibres to be **split into microscopic fibrils** during ballistic impact. The fibrils absorb kinetic energy through a combination of chain stretching, H-bond rupture, and frictional slip — turning the fabric into a "net" that catches a bullet.

---

## 2.3 Thermal & Thermo-Oxidative Stability

Aramids exhibit **no true melting point** because decomposition occurs before melting. The thermal-degradation mechanism in air proceeds in two stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage I (300–400 °C):} \quad & \text{Homolytic scission of the amide C-N bond → formation of aryl radicals + CO} \\
\text{Stage II (400–600 °C):} \quad & \text{Pyrolysis of aromatic rings → char + CO, CO}_2, \text{HCN, H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **char yield at 700 °C** in nitrogen is **~40 %** for both Kevlar and Nomex — a remarkable figure that drives their use in ablative aerospace applications.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — Appear Repeatedly in KTU University Exam**

| # | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carothers Equation (stoichiometric):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = extent of reaction (conversion of functional groups) | Predicts $\bar{X}_n$ when diamine : diacid chloride = 1 : 1 exactly |
| 2 | **Carothers Equation (non-stoichiometric):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | $r$ = mole ratio of minority to majority functional group ($r < 1$) | When slight imbalance (e.g., excess acid chloride) used to control $\bar{X}_n$ |
| 3 | **Number-average degree of polymerisation in A-B type (self-condensing):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Same as #1 — for $\text{AB}$-monomers | For aramids, the actual case is A-A + B-B |
| 4 | **Mark–Houwink Equation:** $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$ | $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity; $K, \alpha$ = polymer-solvent constants | Viscometric determination of molar mass in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ |
| 5 | **Tensile strength relation:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\varepsilon$ = strain | Predicts fibre strength from modulus |
| 6 | **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter relevance:** $\chi = \dfrac{z \cdot \Delta w_{12}}{k_B T}$ | $z$ = lattice coordination, $\Delta w_{12}$ = pair interaction energy | Solvent selection (e.g., why $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ dissolves PPTA but NMP does not) |
| 7 | **Heat resistance index (L.O.I. classification):** $\text{LOI} \geq 28 \Rightarrow$ **self-extinguishing** | Volume-percent O₂ required for sustained combustion | Aramids fall in the "self-extinguishing" regime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall Avoidance (KTU Valuation):**
> When asked *"Why does Kevlar not melt?"*, do not write "because it has crosslinks" — Kevlar is a **linear** polymer with **no crosslinks**. The correct reason is *"Aramids undergo thermal decomposition prior to reaching a melting temperature because the resonance-stabilised aromatic backbone requires bond dissociation energy greater than the thermal energy at the theoretical $T_m$."*

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Industry Sector | Specific Use Case | Aramid Grade |
|---|---|---|
| Defence / Ballistics | Bullet-proof vests, helmets, spall liners | Kevlar 29, 129 |
| Aerospace | Composite reinforcement in aircraft fuselage, ablative heat shields | Kevlar 49 |
| Automotive | Brake-pad reinforcement, clutch facings, tyre cord | Kevlar |
| Electrical | Transformer insulation, motor slot wedges, printed-circuit board substrate | Nomex 410, 414 |
| Fire Safety | Fire-fighter turnout gear, racing-suit fabric, hot-gas filtration bags | Nomex III |
| Telecommunications | Optical-fibre cables (Kevlar as tensile strength member) | Kevlar 49 |
| Civil Engineering | Concrete reinforcement, seismic retrofitting | Kevlar |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Synthesis of Kevlar (PPTA) — Low-Temperature Solution Polycondensation

This is the **industrial "DuPont" route** (Stephens, 1974) and is the most exam-relevant synthesis in KTU PEPOT751.

### 3.1.1 Raw Materials

- **Monomer A (diamine):** *p*-Phenylenediamine (PPD), $\text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}_2$
- **Monomer B (diacid chloride):** Terephthaloyl chloride (TPC), $\text{ClOC}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{COCl}$
- **Solvent system:** *N*-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) + 5–8 % $\text{CaCl}_2$ (Lewis-acid activator) **OR** concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$

### 3.1.2 Balanced Chemical Equation (Stepwise Derivation)

**Step 1 — Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution (Elementary Reaction):**

The amine nitrogen lone pair attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the acid chloride:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2\text{N}-\!\!\underset{1,4}{\text{Ar}}\!\!-\text{NH}_2 \;+\; \text{ClOC}-\!\!\underset{1,4}{\text{Ar}'}\!\!-\text{COCl} \;\xrightarrow{\text{NMP/CaCl}_2, \; T \le 5\,°\text{C}}\; \text{H}-\!\left[\text{NH}-\text{Ar}-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{Ar}'-\text{CO}\right]_n\!\!-\text{Cl} \;+\; (2n-1)\,\text{HCl}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — HCl Neutralisation (Acid-Acceptor Function):**

Because HCl is liberated stoichiometrically, a tertiary amine base (pyridine, triethylamine $\text{Et}_3\text{N}$) is added to scavenge it. Without the base, HCl would protonate the diamine (forming non-nucleophilic $\text{-NH}_3^+$), halting polymerisation. The acid-binding reaction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Et}_3\text{N} \;+\; \text{HCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Et}_3\text{NH}^+\text{Cl}^-
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Full Balanced Overall Equation (Final):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\,\text{H}_2\text{N}-\!\!\underset{1,4}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{NH}_2 \;+\; n\,\text{ClOC}-\!\!\underset{1,4}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{COCl} \;\xrightarrow{\text{NMP + CaCl}_2, \, \text{Et}_3\text{N}}\\ \text{H}-\!\!\left[\text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}\right]_n\!\!-\text{Cl} \;+\; 2n\,\text{Et}_3\text{NHCl}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Why Low Temperature?

The Schotten–Baumann-type condensation is highly exothermic ($\Delta H \approx -110\ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$ of HCl evolved). To obtain **high molar mass** ($\bar{M}_n > 20{,}000\ \text{g·mol}^{-1}$), side reactions (hydrolysis of acid chloride by trace water, premature precipitation of oligomer) must be suppressed. Low temperature ($T \leq 5\ \text{°C}$) and anhydrous conditions are therefore mandatory.

---

## 3.2 Synthesis of Nomex (MPIA) — Interfacial Polycondensation

This route (Morgan, 1965) is the canonical example KTU examiners use to test the student's grasp of **phase-transfer polymerisation**.

### 3.2.1 Procedure (Detailed Stepwise Derivation)

**Step 1 — Two-Phase System Setup:**

| Phase | Composition | Location of Species |
|---|---|---|
| **Aqueous phase** | *m*-Phenylenediamine + $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ (HCl scavenger) | Dissolved MPD migrates toward interface |
| **Organic phase** | Isophthaloyl chloride in $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ or $\text{CCl}_4$ | TPC remains in bulk organic phase |

**Step 2 — Interfacial Condensation at the Phase Boundary:**

At the aqueous/organic interface, MPD diffuses outward and reacts with TPC diffusing inward:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\,\text{H}_2\text{N}-\!\!\underset{1,3}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{NH}_2 \;+\; n\,\text{ClOC}-\!\!\underset{1,3}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{COCl} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \text{ (aq)}/\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2}\\ \text{H}-\!\!\left[\text{NH}-\!\!\underset{1,3}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\!\!\underset{1,3}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{CO}\right]_n\!\!-\text{Cl} \;+\; 2n\,\text{HCl} \;(\text{neutralised by Na}_2\text{CO}_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — HCl Scavenging:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,\text{HCl} \;+\; \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{NaCl} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \text{CO}_2\uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Why Interfacial Polymerisation Yields High $\bar{M}_n$

In interfacial polymerisation, the reaction is **diffusion-controlled**, not kinetically controlled. Each MPD molecule that reaches the interface reacts almost instantly. The byproduct (HCl) diffuses into water and is neutralised. As a result, the **Carothers effective conversion is ~99.95 %**, yielding $\bar{X}_n > 200$ in seconds — a striking example of how reactor engineering (not chemistry) controls molar mass.

---

## 3.3 Application of the Carothers Equation to Aramid Synthesis (Detailed Derivation)

### 3.3.1 Problem Set-Up

Suppose Kevlar is synthesised by reacting 100 mol PPD with 102 mol TPC (slight diacid chloride excess to control $\bar{X}_n$). The desired *number-average* degree of polymerisation $\bar{X}_n$ must be predicted.

**Step 1 — Identify the Limiting Reagent and Calculate $r$:**

The diamine is the minority functional group:
- Total amine equivalents: $2 \times 100 = 200$
- Total acid-chloride equivalents: $2 \times 102 = 204$

Therefore, $r = \dfrac{\text{minority equivalents}}{\text{majority equivalents}} = \dfrac{200}{204} = 0.9804$

**Step 2 — State the Carothers Non-Stoichiometric Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $p = 0.99$ (99 % conversion of acid-chloride groups):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{1 + 0.9804}{1 + 0.9804 - 2(0.9804)(0.99)} \;=\; \dfrac{1.9804}{1.9804 - 1.9412} \;=\; \dfrac{1.9804}{0.0392} \;\approx\; 50.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\bar{M}_n$ (Aramid Repeat-Unit Molar Mass):**

The PPTA repeat unit $-\text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-$ has a molar mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_0 \;=\; (2 \times 14) + (12 \times 6) + (4 \times 1) + 28 + 28 \;=\; 238\ \text{g·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n \;=\; \bar{X}_n \times \bar{M}_0 \;=\; 50.5 \times 238 \;\approx\; 12{,}020\ \text{g·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Industrial Kevlar fibres target $\bar{M}_n \sim 20{,}000$–$40{,}000$ g·mol⁻¹, which requires $r \to 1.000$ and $p \to 0.999$. This is why Kevlar synthesis demands **sublimation-purified PPD** and **recrystallised TPC** — trace impurities catastrophically reduce $p$.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Kevlar vs Nomex (KTU Analytical View)

| Parameter | Kevlar (PPTA, *p*-aramid) | Nomex (MPIA, *m*-aramid) |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer substitution** | 1,4 (linear) | 1,3 (angular) |
| **Chain geometry** | Rigid rod, extended | Semi-flexible, kinked |
| **Crystallinity** | Highly crystalline (lyotropic LC) | Amorphous to semi-crystalline |
| **Synthesis route** | Low-temperature solution polycondensation (NMP/CaCl₂) | Interfacial polycondensation |
| **Decomposition $T_d$** | ~500 °C | ~370 °C |
| **Tensile strength** | 3.6 GPa | 0.5 GPa |
| **Modulus** | 130 GPa | 17 GPa |
| **Primary applications** | Ballistics, composites, ropes | Electrical insulation, fire-suits |
| **Solvent** | Concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ only | DMF, DMAc, NMP with LiCl/CaCl₂ |
| **Density** | 1.44 g·cm⁻³ | 1.38 g·cm⁻³ |
| **LOI** | ~29 % | ~28–30 % |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Aramid Synthesis & Property Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Selection of Diamine + Diacid Chloride] --> B{Ring Substitution Pattern?}
    B -- 1,4 para --> C[Linear Rigid-Rod Chain]
    B -- 1,3 meta --> D[Angular Kinked Chain]
    C --> E[Lyotropic Liquid-Crystal Phase in H2SO4]
    D --> F[Amorphous to Semi-Crystalline Morphology]
    E --> G[Dry-Jet Wet Spinning]
    F --> H[Wet Spinning into Aqueous Coagulation Bath]
    G --> I[Kevlar Fibre]
    H --> J[Nomex Fibre]
    I --> K[Ballistic Composites / Ropes / Tyres]
    J --> L[Electrical Insulation / Fire-Suits / Filters]
    K --> M[High Tensile 3.6 GPa]
    L --> N[High LOI 28 to 30 Percent]
    I --> O[Hydrogen-Bonded Sheet Network]
    J --> P[Discontinuous H-Bond Network]
    O --> Q[Exceptional Axial Modulus]
    P --> R[Good Dielectric Strength]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Kevlar Dry-Jet Wet Spinning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MonomerPPD[para-Phenylenediamine] --> Mix[Dissolve in NMP plus CaCl2]
    MonomerTPC[Terephthaloyl Chloride] --> Mix
    Mix --> Reactor[Polycondensation Reactor 0 to 5 C]
    Reactor --> LC[Lyotropic Polymer Solution 18 to 20 Percent]
    LC --> Spinneret[Spinneret Extrusion Through Air Gap]
    Spinneret --> Gap[Cooled Air Gap Aligns Rods]
    Gap --> Coag[Coagulation Bath Aqueous H2SO4]
    Coag --> Wash[Neutralisation and Washing]
    Wash --> Dry[Drying on Heated Rolls]
    Dry --> Final[Kevlar Continuous Filament Yarn]
```

## 4.3 Mechanism Flow — Schotten-Baumann Condensation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Diamine N-H Attacks Carbonyl C of Acid Chloride] --> StepB[Tetrahedral Intermediate Forms]
    StepB --> StepC[Chloride Ion Leaves, Amide Bond Closes]
    StepC --> StepD[HCl Released]
    StepD --> StepE[Et3N or Na2CO3 Scavenges HCl]
    StepE --> StepF[Propagation Continues at Chain End]
    F1[Para Geometry] --> Linear[Linear Rigid Rod]
    F2[Meta Geometry] --> Kinked[120 Degree Kinked Chain]
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Structure of Kevlar Fibre

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Molecular Level: Para-Substituted Amide Bonds with Colinear Geometry] --> F1[Microfibril Level: H-Bonded Sheets of Aligned Rods]
    F1 --> F2[Fibril Level: Bundle of Microfibrils Held by Van der Waals Forces]
    F2 --> F3[Fibre Level: Twisted Fibril Bundle, 12 Micron Diameter]
    F3 --> F4[Yarn Level: Multi-Filament Twist, 1500 Denier Typical]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in the KTU Exam:**
> A "block diagram" approach (as above) is fully acceptable for KTU answer sheets. Examiners reward **logical flow** and **labelled arrows** — you do **not** need an artistic drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define aromatic polyamides. Give two examples.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aromatic polyamides, popularly known as **aramids**, are a class of high-performance polymers in which the amide linkages ($-\text{CO}-\text{NH}-$) are directly attached to aromatic (benzene) rings. By the FTC definition, at least **85 %** of the amide bonds must be connected to aromatic nuclei. The aromatic rings confer rigidity, thermal stability, and exceptional mechanical strength.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Kevlar** — Poly-*para*-phenylene terephthalamide (PPTA), used in bullet-proof vests and composites.
2. **Nomex** — Poly-*meta*-phenylene isophthalamide (MPIA), used in fire-resistant clothing and electrical insulation paper.

*(1 mark for FTC definition, 1 mark for structural feature, 1 mark for examples with applications.)*

---

### Question 2: Why does Kevlar not melt upon heating?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Kevlar (PPTA) is a **rigid-rod, fully aromatic polymer** whose backbone consists exclusively of conjugated aromatic rings and amide linkages. Three reasons prevent melting:

1. **No chain mobility:** The *para*-substituted geometry forces the chain into a near-linear conformation with essentially **zero conformational freedom**, so no entropy gain accompanies heating (no $\Delta S_{\text{fusion}}$).
2. **Strong intermolecular forces:** Dense, two-dimensional **hydrogen bonding** between amide groups (resonance-strengthened by aromatic conjugation) holds chains rigidly together.
3. **Decomposition precedes fusion:** The bond-dissociation energy of aromatic C–C and C–N bonds exceeds the thermal energy required to reach any theoretical $T_m$. The polymer undergoes **thermo-oxidative degradation at ~500 °C** rather than melting.

Hence Kevlar exhibits *no melting point*, only a *decomposition temperature*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part-B Module-1 questions carry **14 marks** split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the synthesis of Kevlar by low-temperature solution polycondensation. Write the reaction scheme and explain why low temperature is essential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the structure, properties, and applications of Kevlar and Nomex. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Reaction Scheme (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\,\text{H}_2\text{N}-\!\!\underset{1,4}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{NH}_2 \;+\; n\,\text{ClOC}-\!\!\underset{1,4}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{COCl} \;\xrightarrow{\text{NMP + CaCl}_2,\, \text{Et}_3\text{N},\, T \le 5\,°\text{C}}\\ \text{H}-\!\!\left[\text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}\right]_n\!\!-\text{Cl} \;+\; 2n\,\text{Et}_3\text{NHCl}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Procedure (2 marks):**
- Pure *p*-phenylenediamine (PPD) is dissolved in **NMP** containing **5–8 % $\text{CaCl}_2$** as Lewis-acid activator.
- Terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) is added slowly under **stirring at 0–5 °C** under a **dry nitrogen atmosphere**.
- Triethylamine ($\text{Et}_3\text{N}$) acts as **acid acceptor** to neutralise liberated HCl.
- Polymer precipitates as a fibrous, anisotropic mass; washed with water/acetone and vacuum-dried.

**Why Low Temperature (1 mark):**
- The Schotten–Baumann reaction is **highly exothermic** ($\Delta H \approx -110$ kJ·mol⁻¹ HCl).
- Elevated temperature causes **hydrolysis of the acid chloride** by trace moisture (giving terephthalic acid, which cannot react further).
- Side reactions yield **branched/crosslinked gel** and yellow discolouration.
- Low temperature suppresses these side reactions, ensuring **$\bar{M}_n > 20{,}000$ g·mol⁻¹** and uniform fibre properties.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Attribute | Kevlar (PPTA) | Nomex (MPIA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomers** | *p*-Phenylenediamine + Terephthaloyl chloride | *m*-Phenylenediamine + Isophthaloyl chloride |
| **Ring substitution** | 1,4-*para* (linear) | 1,3-*meta* (angular) |
| **Chain geometry** | Rigid rod | Semi-flexible, kinked |
| **Crystallinity** | Highly crystalline, lyotropic LC | Amorphous / semi-crystalline |
| **Tensile strength** | 3.6 GPa | 0.5 GPa |
| **Modulus** | 130 GPa | 17 GPa |
| **$T_d$** | ~500 °C | ~370 °C |
| **Applications** | Ballistic armour, aircraft composites, ropes, tyres, optical-fibre strength members | Electrical insulation paper, firefighter suits, hot-gas filtration, race-car driver apparel |

**Justification (1 mark):**
The *para*-substitution in Kevlar aligns amide groups collinearly, maximising H-bond density in two dimensions and producing a rigid-rod lyotropic phase that converts directly into highly oriented, near-perfect crystalline fibres. The *meta*-substitution in Nomex introduces 120° kinks that disrupt long-range crystalline order, yielding a more ductile but less strong fibre — ideal where thermal/dielectric stability outweighs the need for extreme mechanical strength.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain interfacial polycondensation with reference to the synthesis of Nomex. Mention the role of each phase and the acid acceptor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Carothers equation, calculate the number-average degree of polymerisation $\bar{X}_n$ of an aramid synthesised from 1.00 mol *p*-phenylenediamine and 1.02 mol terephthaloyl chloride at 99 % conversion of the acid-chloride groups. The repeat-unit molar mass $\bar{M}_0 = 238$ g·mol⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Principle (2 marks):**
Interfacial polycondensation is a heterogeneous polymerisation in which the two reactive monomers are dissolved in **two immiscible liquids** (typically water + an organic solvent). Polymerisation occurs **at the liquid–liquid interface**, where the monomers meet.

**Synthesis of Nomex — Phase-by-Phase (4 marks):**

| Phase | Composition | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Aqueous phase** | *m*-Phenylenediamine (MPD) dissolved in water + $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ | Provides the nucleophilic amine; $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ scavenges HCl |
| **Organic phase** | Isophthaloyl chloride (IPC) dissolved in $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ (or $\text{CCl}_4$) | Provides the electrophilic acid chloride |
| **Interface** | Surface of contact between the two liquids | Site of Schotten–Baumann condensation |

**Reaction at Interface:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\,\text{H}_2\text{N}-\!\!\underset{1,3}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{NH}_2 \;+\; n\,\text{ClOC}-\!\!\underset{1,3}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4}\!\!-\text{COCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Poly-}\textit{m}\text{-aramid} \;+\; 2n\,\text{HCl}
\end{aligned}
$$

The HCl is **immediately neutralised** by $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ in the aqueous phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,\text{HCl} \;+\; \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{NaCl} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \text{CO}_2\uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this gives high $\bar{M}_n$ (1 mark):**
Reaction is diffusion-controlled; each MPD molecule reacts almost instantaneously upon reaching the interface. The effective conversion is >99.95 %, so the Carothers $\bar{X}_n$ is very high even with non-stoichiometric feeds.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Full Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Identify $r$ (stoichiometric imbalance ratio):**

The diamine provides $2 \times 1.00 = 2.00$ amine equivalents. The diacid chloride provides $2 \times 1.02 = 2.04$ acid-chloride equivalents. Hence diamine is the **minority**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{minority}}}{N_{\text{majority}}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.00}{2.04} \;=\; 0.9804
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Carothers Non-Stoichiometric Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $r = 0.9804$, $p = 0.99$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{1 + 0.9804}{1 + 0.9804 - 2(0.9804)(0.99)} \;=\; \dfrac{1.9804}{1.9804 - 1.9412} \;=\; \dfrac{1.9804}{0.0392}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n \;\approx\; 50.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\bar{M}_n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n \;=\; \bar{X}_n \times \bar{M}_0 \;=\; 50.5 \times 238 \;\approx\; 12{,}019\ \text{g·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):**
The $\bar{M}_n$ obtained is **adequate for fibre spinning** but **below the industrial target** ($>20{,}000$ g·mol⁻¹). To raise $\bar{M}_n$, one must (i) increase stoichiometric purity to push $r \to 1.000$, and (ii) drive conversion $p \to 0.999$ by removing every trace of moisture and impurities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Aromatic Polyamide Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Kevlar & Nomex substitutions** — writing *"Kevlar is meta-substituted"* costs the full structural-mark allocation. Always draw the ring and label the 1,4 vs 1,3 connectivity explicitly.
> 2. **Citing "crosslinking" as the reason for non-melting** — Kevlar is **linear**. The correct reason is **chain rigidity + decomposition preceding fusion**.
> 3. **Omitting the acid-acceptor role** in polycondensation equations — $\text{Et}_3\text{N}$ / $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ is **mandatory** in the balanced equation. Loss: 1 mark per scheme.
> 4. **Writing "$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is released"** — Kevlar/Nomex synthesis releases **HCl, not $\text{H}_2\text{O}$** (acid chloride + amine, not carboxylic acid + amine). A common and costly error.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in Carothers calculations — write $\bar{M}_n = 12{,}019\ \text{g·mol}^{-1}$, not just "12,019".
> 6. **Missing the engineering interpretation** at the end of a numerical problem — KTU 2024 scheme rewards the **"why does this number matter?"** sentence. Always close with a one-line application context.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aromatic Polyamides (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Aramids = polyamides with ≥ 85 % amide bonds attached to aromatic rings (FTC).
>
> ✅ **Two flagship products:** **Kevlar (PPTA, 1,4-*para*)** and **Nomex (MPIA, 1,3-*meta*)**.
>
> ✅ **Kevlar synthesis:** *p*-phenylenediamine + terephthaloyl chloride in **NMP + CaCl₂ + Et₃N** at **0–5 °C**; byproduct is **HCl** (not water).
>
> ✅ **Nomex synthesis:** *m*-phenylenediamine (aq) + isophthaloyl chloride (org) via **interfacial polycondensation** with **Na₂CO₃** as HCl scavenger.
>
> ✅ **Why Kevlar is rigid-rod:** *para*-substitution → colinear chain → extended conformation → lyotropic liquid crystal in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.
>
> ✅ **Why Kevlar doesn't melt:** Aromatic backbone + 2D H-bonded sheets + decomposition precedes fusion (no $T_m$, only $T_d \sim 500\ \text{°C}$).
>
> ✅ **Hydrogen bonding:** Resonance-strengthened by aromatic conjugation; bond energy ≈ 20–40 kJ·mol⁻¹ (≈ 3–4× stronger than in aliphatic nylons).
>
> ✅ **Carothers (non-stoichiometric):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$; always identify the **minority functional group** first.
>
> ✅ **Mark–Houwink:** $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$ — use concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ as solvent for PPTA viscometry.
>
> ✅ **Performance benchmark numbers to memorise:** Kevlar strength 3.6 GPa, modulus 130 GPa, density 1.44 g·cm⁻³, LOI ~29.
>
> ✅ **Applications at a glance:** Kevlar → ballistics, composites, tyres, ropes; Nomex → electrical insulation, fire-suits, filtration.
>
> ✅ **Pitfall phrases to AVOID in exam:** "crosslinked", "melts at 500 °C", "byproduct water", "Kevlar is meta-substituted", "Nomex is rigid-rod".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyketones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT751 - SPECIALITY POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polyketones

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polyketones** are a class of high-performance speciality polymers characterized by the presence of **carbonyl groups (–C(=O)–)** integrated directly into the polymer backbone, typically in conjunction with **aryl (aromatic) or ether linkages**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT751) definition, polyketones are engineering thermoplastics in which aromatic rings are connected through ether and ketone linkages, conferring exceptional thermal stability, mechanical strength, and chemical resistance.

The general structural representation is:

$$\text{–[Ar–O–Ar–C(=O)–]}_{n}$$

where **Ar** denotes an aromatic ring (commonly *para*-substituted phenylene).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Polyketones fall under the broader family of **Polyaryletherketones (PAEKs)**, which are recognized as **super-engineering thermoplastics** alongside PEEK, PEK, PEKK, and PEEKK.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Polyketones (CO1 – KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Aromatic Polyketones (PAEK family):** PEEK, PEK, PEKK, PEEKK
> 2. **Aliphatic Polyketones:** Perfectly alternating ethylene–CO copolymers (Carilon / Shell)
> 3. **Partially Aromatic / Aliphatic–Aromatic Hybrids**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a polyketone chain like a **reinforced steel bridge structure**:

- The **aromatic benzene rings** act like the strong, rigid trusses.
- The **ether linkages (–O–)** function as the flexible, hinge-like connecting joints that allow controlled movement.
- The **carbonyl groups (C=O)** act as the load-bearing pillars that resist thermal and chemical stress.

Just as a bridge with a balanced combination of rigid trusses, flexible joints, and load-bearing pillars can withstand hurricanes, earthquakes, and heavy traffic, a polyketone polymer with aromatic rings, ether bonds, and ketone groups can resist high temperatures (above **300 °C**), aggressive chemicals, and mechanical deformation.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics for Polyketones:**
> - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** **143 °C** (for PEEK)
> - **Melting Temperature (Tm):** **343 °C** (for PEEK)
> - **Continuous Use Temperature:** **260 °C**
> - **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI):** **35%**
> - **Young's Modulus:** **3.6 GPa**
> - **Tensile Strength:** **100 MPa**
> - **Dielectric Strength:** **190 kV/mm**

---

## 1.4 Commercial Significance

Polyketones are positioned at the **top of the engineering thermoplastics pyramid**, often competing with metals and ceramics. Their high cost (₹₹₹) is justified by performance in:
- Aerospace composites
- Medical implants (biocompatible)
- Semiconductor wafer carriers
- Oil & gas downhole components

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polyketones – Detailed Breakdown

### A. Based on Backbone Composition

| Class | Example | Repeat Unit | Synthesis Route |
|-------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| Polyetheretherketone (PEEK) | Victrex PEEK | –O–Ar–O–Ar–C(=O)–Ar– | Nucleophilic (bisphenol + difluoroketone) |
| Polyetherketone (PEK) | – | –O–Ar–C(=O)–Ar– | Electrophilic (Friedel–Crafts) |
| Polyetherketoneketone (PEKK) | – | –O–Ar–C(=O)–Ar–C(=O)–Ar– | Electrophilic |
| Polyetheretherketoneketone (PEEKK) | – | –O–Ar–O–Ar–C(=O)–Ar–C(=O)–Ar– | Nucleophilic |
| Aliphatic Polyketone | Carilon | –(CH₂–CH₂–C=O)ₙ– | Palladium-catalyzed CO/ethylene |

### B. Structural Role of Each Linkage

1. **Aromatic Rings (–Ar–):**
   - Provide **rigidity** and **thermal stability**.
   - Contribute to π-electron delocalization, enhancing mechanical strength.
   - Raise Tg and Tm significantly.

2. **Ether Linkages (–O–):**
   - Impart **chain flexibility**, improving processability.
   - Lower the melt viscosity compared to fully aromatic polymers.
   - Provide rotational freedom around the C–O–C bond.

3. **Ketone Linkages (–C(=O)–):**
   - The **polar carbonyl group** contributes to:
     - High inter-chain dipole interactions
     - Excellent chemical resistance
     - Flame retardancy (char formation)
   - Increases the **electron-withdrawing character**, which is critical for nucleophilic substitution synthesis.

---

## 2.2 Synthesis Mechanisms (High-Yield KTU Focus)

### Route 1: Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution (For PEEK)

$$\text{HO–Ar–OH} + \text{F–Ar–C(=O)–Ar–F} \xrightarrow{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{DMAc}, \Delta} \text{–[O–Ar–O–Ar–C(=O)–Ar–]}_{n}$$

**Mechanism (SNAr):**
1. Deprotonation of bisphenol by K₂CO₃ forms the **phenoxide nucleophile**.
2. The phenoxide attacks the **ipso-carbon** bearing fluorine (activated by the *para* ketone).
3. A **Meisenheimer complex** (anionic σ-complex) is formed as the rate-determining step.
4. Loss of fluoride restores aromaticity, forming the ether linkage.

### Route 2: Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (Friedel–Crafts, For PEK/PEKK)

$$\text{Ar–H} + \text{Cl–C(=O)–Ar–C(=O)–Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{AlCl}_3, \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2} \text{PEKK}$$

**Key requirements:**
- Use of **electron-rich aromatic** (diphenyl ether) and **electron-poor acyl chloride**.
- Anhydrous conditions to prevent hydrolysis of AlCl₃.

### Route 3: Palladium-Catalyzed CO/Ethylene Copolymerization (Aliphatic Polyketone)

$$n\,\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Pd(II)/phosphine, CH}_3\text{OH}} \text{–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–C=O)}_{n}$$

This is a **perfectly alternating** copolymer (no random sequences) due to the strict chain-termination mechanism of the Pd catalyst.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE – KTU Board Exam Ready**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---------|---------------------|-------|-------------|
| Glass Transition (Fox equation) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | K | Blend Tg prediction |
| Mark–Houwink | $[\eta] = K M_a^{\alpha}$ | dL/g | Molecular weight |
| Thermal Expansion | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \Delta T}$ | K⁻¹ | Dimensional stability |
| Char Yield (TGA) | $\% \text{Char} = \dfrac{W_{900}}{W_0} \times 100$ | % | Flame retardancy |
| Heat Deflection Temp | $\text{HDT} = 0.33 \times T_m + 0.67 \times T_g$ | °C | Load-bearing limit |
| Dielectric Loss | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | dimensionless | Electronic grade |
| Crystallinity (XRD) | $X_c = \dfrac{A_{cryst}}{A_{cryst} + A_{amorph}} \times 100$ | % | Processing control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Relationship:** The **ketone-to-ether ratio (K/E ratio)** is the single most important design parameter:
> - **Higher K/E** → higher Tg, Tm, stiffness, but **lower processability**.
> - **Lower K/E** (e.g., PEEK with 1:2 ratio) → easier melt processing, better toughness.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Why Polyketone? |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| Aerospace | Wing leading edges, fairings | Lightweight + 260 °C continuous service |
| Medical | Spinal cages, dental implants | Biocompatible, sterilizable (autoclave) |
| Electronics | Wafer carriers, connectors | Ultra-low outgassing, halogen-free |
| Oil & Gas | Compressor rings, seals | Chemical + steam resistance |
| Automotive | Piston skirts, bearings | Low friction, high PV limit |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT) for a Polyketone

The Heat Deflection Temperature is the temperature at which a standard polymer bar deflects by **0.25 mm** under a load of **0.45 MPa or 1.82 MPa** (ASTM D648). A semi-empirical relationship commonly used for semi-crystalline polyketones is:

$$\text{HDT} \approx a \cdot T_m + b \cdot T_g$$

For PEEK (Tm = 343 °C, Tg = 143 °C) under 1.82 MPa fiber stress:

**Step 1:** Identify empirical coefficients for semi-crystalline engineering thermoplastics.
$$a = 0.33, \quad b = 0.67$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values (temperatures in °C).
$$\text{HDT}_{1.82} = (0.33 \times 343) + (0.67 \times 143)$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term explicitly.
$$\text{HDT}_{1.82} = 113.19 + 95.81$$

**Step 4:** Add the terms.
$$\text{HDT}_{1.82} = 209.0 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with experimental value (≈ 160 °C at 1.82 MPa for PEEK). The deviation (~30 °C) arises because HDT depends on the **modulus-temperature curve**, not just Tg and Tm — the empirical coefficients vary with crystallinity.

$$\text{HDT}_{0.45} = (0.33 \times 343) + (0.67 \times 143) \approx 240 \text{ °C (low-stress condition)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Molecular Weight from Mark–Houwink Equation

For PEEK in concentrated H₂SO₄ at 25 °C, the Mark–Houwink parameters are:
$$K = 4.13 \times 10^{-4} \text{ dL/g}, \quad \alpha = 0.78$$

**Step 1:** Intrinsic viscosity measured: $[\eta] = 1.20 \text{ dL/g}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the Mark–Houwink equation.
$$[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha} \implies 1.20 = (4.13 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot M_v^{0.78}$$

**Step 3:** Isolate $M_v^{0.78}$.
$$M_v^{0.78} = \dfrac{1.20}{4.13 \times 10^{-4}} = 2905.57$$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm to linearize.
$$0.78 \cdot \ln M_v = \ln(2905.57) = 7.975$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\ln M_v$.
$$\ln M_v = \dfrac{7.975}{0.78} = 10.224$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate to find $M_v$.
$$M_v = e^{10.224} = 2.77 \times 10^{4} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Result:** The viscosity-average molecular weight is approximately **27,700 g/mol**, suitable for injection molding of PEEK.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: K/E Ratio vs Properties

| Property | K/E = 1:2 (PEEK) | K/E = 1:1 (PEK) | K/E = 2:1 (PEKK) |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Tg (°C) | 143 | 152 | 165 |
| Tm (°C) | 343 | 367 | 388 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 100 | 110 | 120 |
| Melt Viscosity (Pa·s @ 400 °C) | 350 | 500 | 850 |
| Chemical Resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Superior |
| Processability | Good | Moderate | Difficult |
| Cost | Medium | High | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Rule:** For applications requiring **> 250 °C continuous service** with **complex part geometry**, prefer **PEEK (K/E = 1:2)**. For high-temperature **unfilled sheets or compression molding**, prefer **PEKK**.

---

## 3.4 Quantitative Comparison: PEEK vs PPS vs PEI

| Parameter | PEEK | PPS (Ryton) | PEI (Ultem) |
|-----------|------|-------------|-------------|
| Tg (°C) | **143** | 85 | 217 |
| Tm (°C) | **343** | 280 | Amorphous |
| Continuous Use (°C) | **260** | 200 | 170 |
| Tensile (MPa) | 100 | 75 | 105 |
| LOI (%) | **35** | 47 | 47 |
| Cost (₹/kg) | 4500 | 900 | 2200 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** PEEK offers the best **balance** of high Tg, high Tm, and toughness. PPS is cheaper but lower Tg; PEI has higher Tg but is amorphous (lower solvent resistance).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define polyketones. Give two examples with their applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Polyketones are high-performance engineering thermoplastics containing **carbonyl groups (–C=O–)** in the polymer backbone along with aromatic rings and ether linkages.

**Examples:**
- **PEEK (Polyetheretherketone):** Used in aerospace structural components and medical implants.
- **Aliphatic Polyketone (Carilon):** Used in automotive fuel systems and packaging.

(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 × 1 mark for example-application pairs)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the ketone group in the properties of polyketones?**

**Model Answer:**
The ketone group (–C=O–) is highly **polar**, which contributes to:
1. Strong **inter-chain dipole interactions** → high Tg and tensile strength.
2. **Electron-withdrawing activation** of aromatic rings toward nucleophilic substitution synthesis.
3. **Char formation** on heating → inherent flame retardancy (high LOI).
4. **Resistance to solvents and hydrolysis** due to restricted electron density on the ring.

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B – 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) – Module 1 Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **nucleophilic synthesis route** for PEEK with a suitable reaction scheme. Discuss the role of K₂CO₃ and the solvent DMAc. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **thermal, mechanical, and chemical properties** of PEEK, PEK, and PEKK using a tabular analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION – Question A

#### (a) Nucleophilic Synthesis of PEEK – Step-by-Step

**Step 1 (1 mark) – Reactants:**
- **4,4'-Difluorobenzophenone** (electrophile, fluorine activated by *para* ketone)
- **Hydroquinone** (bisphenol nucleophile)

**Step 2 (2 marks) – Reaction Scheme:**

$$n\,\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} + n\,\text{F–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–C(=O)–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–F} \xrightarrow{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{DMAc, } 180-220\text{°C}} \text{PEEK} + n\,\text{KF} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) – Role of K₂CO₃:**
- Acts as a **mild base** to deprotonate hydroquinone → forms potassium phenoxide.
- The phenoxide is a **stronger nucleophile** than neutral hydroquinone.
- The released HF is neutralized as KF + H₂O (drives equilibrium forward via Le Chatelier).

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) – Role of DMAc (N,N-Dimethylacetamide):**
- **Polar aprotic solvent** that dissolves both monomers and growing polymer.
- Does **not donate protons** (avoids protonating the phenoxide nucleophile).
- High boiling point (~165 °C) permits the high reaction temperature needed.

**Step 5 (1 mark) – Mechanism Summary:**
1. K₂CO₃ deprotonates Ar–OH → ArO⁻K⁺
2. ArO⁻ attacks C–F carbon (ipso position) → Meisenheimer complex
3. Loss of F⁻ → ether linkage formed
4. Repeat to build the chain

#### (b) Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Property | PEEK | PEK | PEKK |
|----------|------|-----|------|
| K/E ratio | 1:2 | 1:1 | 2:1 |
| Tg (°C) | 143 | 152 | 165 |
| Tm (°C) | 343 | 367 | 388 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 100 | 110 | 120 |
| Modulus (GPa) | 3.6 | 4.0 | 4.5 |
| Solvent Resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Superior |
| Processability | Good (melt) | Moderate (compression) | Difficult |
| Cost (relative) | Medium | High | Very High |

**Concluding Remark (1 mark):** As the K/E ratio increases, **thermal and chemical properties improve at the cost of processability**. PEEK remains the commercial workhorse due to the optimal balance.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Module 1 Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **palladium-catalyzed synthesis of aliphatic polyketones** from carbon monoxide and ethylene. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **structure–property relationships** in polyketones. Explain how the K/E ratio affects Tg, Tm, and solvent resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION – Question B

#### (a) Palladium-Catalyzed Aliphatic Polyketone Synthesis (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) – Reaction Scheme:**

$$n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \xrightarrow[\text{CH}_3\text{OH, 90 °C, 6 MPa}]{\text{Pd(OAc)}_2, \text{PPh}_3, \text{bidentate phosphine}} \text{–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–C=O)}_{n}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) – Catalyst System:**
- **Pd(OAc)₂** is the pre-catalyst; reduced in situ to active **Pd(0)**.
- **Phosphine ligands** (often bidentate, e.g., DPPB) stabilize Pd and prevent Pd-black precipitation.
- A **co-catalyst acid** (e.g., p-toluenesulfonic acid) promotes the chain initiation step.

**Step 3 (2 marks) – Mechanism (Cossee–Arlman):**
1. **Oxidative addition:** Pd(0) inserts into H–Pd bond after methanolysis.
2. **CO insertion:** CO coordinates to Pd and migrates into the Pd–C bond.
3. **Ethylene insertion:** CH₂=CH₂ coordinates and inserts alternately.
4. **Chain termination:** β-hydride elimination or protonolysis by methanol gives the polymer + active Pd–H.

**Step 4 (1 mark) – Why Perfectly Alternating?**
- The Pd–acyl intermediate strongly prefers ethylene insertion over CO–CO coupling.
- This gives **> 99% perfectly alternating** structure (no –CO–CO– or –CH₂–CH₂– defects).
- Melt temperature ≈ **220 °C**; density ≈ **1.24 g/cm³**.

**Step 5 (1 mark) – Commercial Note:**
- Developed by **Shell** under the trade name **Carilon** (now produced by Hyosung as **Poketone**).
- Used in fuel-line connectors and packaging.

#### (b) Structure–Property Relationships (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) – Effect on Tg:**
- Tg scales directly with the **K/E ratio**.
- Higher ketone content → higher chain rigidity and dipole interactions.
- **PEEK (K/E=1:2):** Tg = 143 °C; **PEKK (K/E=2:1):** Tg = 165 °C.
- Equation: $T_g \propto \rho_{carbonyl}$ (carbonyl density).

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) – Effect on Tm:**
- Tm also increases with K/E.
- **PEEK:** 343 °C; **PEKK:** 388 °C.
- Reason: stronger inter-chain forces (Keesom dipole–dipole) require more thermal energy to disrupt the crystal lattice.

**Step 3 (2 marks) – Effect on Solvent Resistance:**
- The polar C=O group **activates the aromatic ring** against electrophilic attack, making it less reactive.
- High crystallinity (typically 30–35%) blocks solvent diffusion.
- PEEK is resistant to: ketones, esters, ethers, hydrocarbons, acids (except conc. H₂SO₄).
- PEKK > PEEK in resistance due to higher K/E and crystallinity.

**Step 4 (1 mark) – Other Property Trends:**
- **Tensile strength:** ↑ with K/E (PEKK > PEK > PEEK).
- **Melt viscosity:** ↑ with K/E (PEKK hardest to process).
- **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI):** ↑ with K/E (PEKK ≈ 40, PEEK ≈ 35).

**Step 5 (1 mark) – Engineering Trade-off Summary:**
$$\text{Performance} \uparrow \;\;\; \Longleftrightarrow \;\;\; \text{Processability} \downarrow$$

This trade-off defines the choice between PEEK, PEK, and PEKK in real applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Polyketone Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PEEK with PEK:** PEEK has TWO ether and ONE ketone per repeat unit. PEK has ONE ether and ONE ketone. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for swapping.
> 2. **Wrong mechanism:** Students often write *electrophilic* substitution for PEEK synthesis. PEEK is synthesized by **nucleophilic** (SNAr) route. PEK/PEKK use **electrophilic** (Friedel–Crafts).
> 3. **Missing K₂CO₃ role:** Must explicitly state it deprotonates the bisphenol to form the phenoxide nucleophile.
> 4. **Ignoring DMAc nature:** Must specify **polar aprotic**; protic solvents destroy the phenoxide.
> 5. **No mention of alternating structure:** Aliphatic polyketones are **perfectly alternating** CO–ethylene due to Pd catalyst. Writing "random copolymer" loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Always quote Tg in °C, modulus in GPa, and LOI in %. Examiners often check units in the final year.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST – Polyketones**
> - ☐ Polyketones = polymers with **–C(=O)–** groups in the backbone.
> - ☐ **PEEK** repeat unit: **–O–C₆H₄–O–C₆H₄–C(=O)–C₆H₄–**
> - ☐ **PEK** repeat unit: **–O–C₆H₄–C(=O)–C₆H₄–**
> - ☐ **PEKK** repeat unit: **–O–C₆H₄–C(=O)–C₆H₄–C(=O)–C₆H₄–**
> - ☐ **Aliphatic polyketone:** perfectly alternating **–(CH₂CH₂–CO)ₙ–**
> - ☐ PEEK Tg = **143 °C**, Tm = **343 °C**, continuous use = **260 °C**.
> - ☐ PEEK is synthesized by **nucleophilic SNAr** with K₂CO₃ in DMAc.
> - ☐ Aliphatic polyketone uses **Pd/phosphine catalyst** with CO + ethylene.
> - ☐ **K/E ratio** is the master design parameter.
> - ☐ Higher K/E → higher Tg, Tm, solvent resistance, but harder to process.
> - ☐ Applications: aerospace, medical implants, semiconductor, oil & gas.
> - ☐ Trade names: **Victrex PEEK**, **Carilon/Poketone** (aliphatic).
> - ☐ LOI of PEEK = **35%** → self-extinguishing.
> - ☐ PEEK is **semi-crystalline** (~30–35% crystallinity).
> - ☐ Solvent of choice for GPC: **concentrated H₂SO₄**.

**End of Topic: Polyketones | Module 1 | PEPOT751**
