**(a)** Explain, with suitable examples, the **Eisenberg–Hird–Moore (EHM) cluster model** for polyaromatic ionomers. Discuss how the **nano-phase separation** between ionic clusters and the hydrophobic matrix gives rise to simultaneous mechanical strength and ionic conductivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) **SPEEK** sample has IEC = 1.85 meq/g. The repeat unit of PEEK is **-C₁₉H₁₂O₃-** (M = 288.3 g/mol). Calculate:
- (i) the **degree of sulfonation (DS)**,
- (ii) the **mass of -SO₃H groups** in 1 kg of dry polymer,
- (iii) the **water uptake** if a 1.000 g sample absorbs 0.280 g of water at equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Option A

**(a) EHM Cluster Model — 7 marks**

*(1 mark for concept statement)* The EHM model, proposed in 1990 by Eisenberg, Hird, and Moore, is the canonical explanation for ionomer behavior.

*(2 marks for mechanism)* Three structural tiers are recognised:

- **Multiplets (≤ 1 nm):** Tightly packed aggregates of 2–8 ion pairs held by **Coulombic forces**, with no polymer chain segment inside them. They behave as **physical crosslinks**, raising $T_g$ and modulus.
- **Clusters (2–5 nm):** In the presence of even trace moisture, multiplets coalesce into **hydrophilic cluster regions**. Polymer chains thread through them, with reduced mobility near the cluster boundary.
- **Matrix:** The bulk polyaromatic phase, largely unaffected by the ionic groups.

*(2 marks for property dualism)* The **nano-phase separation** between clusters (ionic, hydrophilic, conductive) and matrix (hydrophobic, mechanically strong) explains:
- **Mechanical strength** → arises from multiplet-mediated physical crosslinks in the dry matrix.
- **Ionic conductivity** → arises from continuous **cluster-channel networks** that percolate water and protons.
- As temperature or humidity rises, clusters grow and interconnect, producing a **conductivity jump** at the **cluster percolation threshold**.

*(1 mark for examples and applications)* SPEEK and Nafion are textbook examples; SPEEK is cheaper, more eco-friendly, and operates up to 80 °C in PEMFCs, whereas Nafion excels at low humidity.

*(1 mark for synthesis-application link)* Controlled sulfonation is the engineering lever — tune DS to position the material near (but not above) the percolation threshold for optimum conductivity without sacrificing toughness.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given data:**
- $IEC = 1.85 \text{ meq/g}$
- $M_{PEEK} = 288.3 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{SO_3H} = 81.07 \text{ g/mol}$

**(i) Degree of Sulfonation (3 marks)**

**Step 1 — Theoretical equivalent mass at full sulfonation (DS = 1):**
$$M_{eq}^{theor} = M_{PEEK} + M_{SO_3H} - M_H = 288.3 + 81.07 - 1.008$$
$$M_{eq}^{theor} = 368.36 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 2 — Actual equivalent mass from IEC:**
$$M_{eq}^{actual} = \dfrac{1000}{IEC} = \dfrac{1000}{1.85} = 540.54 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 3 — Compute DS:**
$$DS = \dfrac{M_{eq}^{theor}}{M_{eq}^{actual}} = \dfrac{368.36}{540.54}$$
$$\boxed{DS = 0.6816 \approx 0.682 \text{ or } 68.2\%}$$

---

**(ii) Mass of -SO₃H groups in 1 kg dry polymer (2 marks)**

**Step 1 — Moles of -SO₃H per kg:**
$$n_{SO_3H} = IEC \times 1000 \text{ g} = 1.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ eq/g} \times 1000 \text{ g} = 1.85 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of -SO₃H groups:**
$$m_{SO_3H} = n_{SO_3H} \times M_{SO_3H} = 1.85 \times 81.07$$
$$\boxed{m_{SO_3H} = 149.98 \text{ g} \approx 150.0 \text{ g per kg dry SPEEK}}$$

---

**(iii) Water Uptake (2 marks)**

$$WU = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} = \dfrac{0.280}{1.000} = 0.280$$
$$\boxed{WU = 28.0\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on (iii):** A WU of 28 % falls within the **optimum fuel-cell window (25–35 %)** — this sample is well-suited for PEMFC application.

---

### Question B — Option B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Classify ion-exchange resins. Discuss the preparation, properties, and applications of **strong-acid cation (SAC) exchange resins** based on sulfonated polystyrene crosslinked with divinylbenzene (DVB). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2.000 g sample of strong-acid cation resin in **H⁺ form** is contacted with 250.0 mL of **0.0400 M CaCl₂** solution. After 24 h, the supernatant is titrated and the residual [Ca²⁺] is **0.0200 M**, while [H⁺] is **0.0400 M**. Calculate:
- (i) the **selectivity coefficient** $K_{H^+}^{Ca^{2+}}$,
- (ii) the **IEC** of the resin. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Option B

**(a) Classification + SAC Resin Discussion — 7 marks**

*(2 marks for classification)* **Classification of ion-exchange resins:**

| Class | Functional Group | pH Range | Counter-Ion |
|-------|------------------|----------|-------------|
| SAC | -SO₃⁻ | 0–14 | H⁺, Na⁺ |
| WAC | -COO⁻ | 5–14 | H⁺, Na⁺ |
| SBA | -N⁺(CH₃)₃ | 0–14 | OH⁻, Cl⁻ |
| WBA | -NR₂, -NH₂ | 0–7 | Cl⁻ |

*(2 marks for preparation)* **Preparation of SAC resin:**
1. Suspension copolymerize **styrene + divinylbenzene (DVB, 8 %)** in water using benzoyl peroxide initiator → crosslinked polystyrene beads.
2. Sulfonate the beads with **concentrated H₂SO₄ (98 %)** or **oleum (SO₃ in H₂SO₄)** at 80–120 °C for several hours.
3. Wash beads thoroughly until rinse water is neutral; convert to H⁺ form with dilute HCl.

*(2 marks for properties)* **Properties:**
- Spherical beads (0.3–1.2 mm diameter).
- High capacity (4.5–5.0 meq/g dry resin).
- Thermally stable up to ~120 °C; chemically resistant to acids, bases, and oxidants within operating pH range.
- Crosslink density (4–10 % DVB) controls swelling vs. kinetics.

*(1 mark for applications)* **Applications:** domestic water softening, nuclear-waste treatment, catalytic esterification, demineralization (in tandem with SBA resin), pharmaceutical purification.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Initial moles of Ca²⁺ and Cl⁻:**
$$n_{Ca^{2+}}^{initial} = 0.0400 \times 0.2500 = 0.01000 \text{ mol}$$
$$n_{Cl^-}^{initial} = 2 \times n_{Ca^{2+}} = 0.02000 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Final moles (from titration data):**
$$n_{Ca^{2+}}^{final} = 0.0200 \times 0.2500 = 0.005000 \text{ mol}$$
$$n_{H^+}^{final} = 0.0400 \times 0.2500 = 0.01000 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Amount of Ca²⁺ taken up by resin:**
$$n_{Ca^{2+}}^{resin} = 0.01000 - 0.005000 = 0.005000 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Resin-phase equivalents (per g of dry resin):**
Because each Ca²⁺ exchanges for 2 H⁺, equivalents exchanged:
$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = 0.005000 \times 2 = 0.01000 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 5 — Resin-phase concentration of Ca²⁺:**
$$[Ca^{2+}]_r = \dfrac{0.005000 \text{ mol}}{2.000 \text{ g}} = 2.500 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/g}$$
$$[H^+]_r = \dfrac{0.01000 \text{ mol (released)}}{2.000 \text{ g}} = 5.000 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/g}$$

**Step 6 — Apply uni-bivalent selectivity formula:**
$$K_{H^+}^{Ca^{2+}} = \dfrac{[Ca^{2+}]_r \cdot [H^+]_s^{\,2}}{[H^+]_r^{\,2} \cdot [Ca^{2+}]_s}$$
$$K_{H^+}^{Ca^{2+}} = \dfrac{(2.500 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.0400)^2}{(5.000 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot (0.0200)}$$

Numerator: $2.500 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.600 \times 10^{-3} = 4.000 \times 10^{-6}$

Denominator: $25.00 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0200 = 5.000 \times 10^{-7}$

$$K_{H^+}^{Ca^{2+}} = \dfrac{4.000 \times 10^{-6}}{5.000 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.000$$
$$\boxed{K_{H^+}^{Ca^{2+}} = 8.00}$$

---

**(ii) IEC calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Equivalents of H⁺ originally on resin:**
Because all Ca²⁺ uptake is balanced by H⁺ release:
$$n_{H^+}^{resin,\,initial} = n_{H^+}^{released} = 0.01000 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 2 — IEC per gram of dry resin:**
$$IEC = \dfrac{n_{H^+}^{initial}}{m_{dry}} = \dfrac{0.01000 \text{ eq}}{2.000 \text{ g}} = 5.000 \times 10^{-3} \text{ eq/g}$$
$$\boxed{IEC = 5.00 \text{ meq/g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** This IEC (5.00 meq/g) lies within the standard commercial SAC range of **4.5–5.0 meq/g** — a powerful internal sanity check.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Molar-mass confusion (–2 marks per error):** Mixing up the molar mass of the *unsubstituted* repeat unit with that of the *sulfonated* repeat unit when calculating DS. Always use the **unsubstituted** $M_{repeat}$ in the base formula and add the SO₃H correction separately.
>
> 2. **Charge balance ignored (–1 to –2 marks):** When Ca²⁺ exchanges for H⁺, students often write $[Ca]_r = [H^+]_s$ without the **stoichiometric factor of 2**. Recall: 1 mole Ca²⁺ ≡ 2 moles H⁺.
>
> 3. **Units in IEC (–1 mark):** Writing IEC in mol/g instead of meq/g (or mmol/g). Examiners expect the **milli-equivalent** convention, since IEC values are typically fractional when expressed in eq/g.
>
> 4. **Confusing ionomer with polyelectrolyte (–2 marks):** These are **not interchangeable**. State the **ionic content threshold (< 15 mol %)** explicitly when distinguishing.
>
> 5. **Skipping the cluster-network explanation (–1 mark):** When asked about conductivity, merely stating "more SO₃H = more H⁺" loses marks. You must explain the **percolation of ionic clusters** that creates continuous water/proton pathways (EHM model).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Topic: Polyaromatic Ionomers & Polyelectrolyte Ion Exchangers**

### Definition Anchors
- ☐ **Ionomer** = polymer with < 15 mol % ionic groups covalently bound to a hydrophobic backbone.
- ☐ **Polyelectrolyte (ion exchanger)** = crosslinked polymer matrix bearing electrolyte groups (–SO₃H, –COOH, –N⁺R₃, –NH₂) that release/uptake counter-ions in stoichiometric exchange.
- ☐ **Polyaromatic backbone** = main chain built from aromatic (benzenoid/heterocyclic) rings; confers thermal and chemical robustness.

### Structural Hierarchy (EHM Model)
- ☐ Level 1: Polyaromatic chain (matrix).
- ☐ Level 2: Ionic pendants (–SO₃H, –COOH, –PO₃H₂).
- ☐ Level 3: Multiplets (2–8 ion pairs, ≤ 1 nm).
- ☐ Level 4: Clusters (2–5 nm) → percolating ion-conducting channels.

### Classification of Ion Exchangers
- ☐ SAC: –SO₃⁻ — universal cation exchange.
- ☐ WAC: –COO⁻ — selective heavy-metal binding.
- ☐ SBA: –N⁺(CH₃)₃ — universal anion exchange.
- ☐ WBA: –NH₂ / –NR₂ — acid-anion binding.
- ☐ Amphoteric / Chelating: dual-function or selective metal chelation.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- ☐ $IEC = \dfrac{C_{NaOH} \cdot V_{NaOH}}{m_{dry}}$  (meq/g)
- ☐ $WU = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}}$  (dimensionless or %)
- ☐ $K_{H^+}^{Ca^{2+}} = \dfrac{[Ca^{2+}]_r \cdot [H^+]_s^{\,2}}{[H^+]_r^{\,2} \cdot [Ca^{2+}]_s}$
- ☐ $DS = \dfrac{M_{eq}^{theor}}{M_{eq}^{actual}}$;  $M_{eq} = \dfrac{1000}{IEC}$

### Engineering Applications (high-yield facts)
- ☐ **SPS-DVB** → domestic water softeners, nuclear waste treatment.
- ☐ **SPEEK** → PEMFC proton-exchange membranes.
- ☐ **Sulfonated PPO** → catalysis, gas separation.
- ☐ **Sulfonated polyimides** → high-temperature PEMs (> 80 °C operation).
- ☐ **Chelating resins (iminodiacetate-based polyaromatics)** → hydrometallurgy of Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺, Cd²⁺.

### Critical Trade-Offs (likely viva question)
- ☐ ↑ DS → ↑ conductivity BUT ↓ mechanical stability (excess swelling).
- ☐ ↑ Crosslink (DVB %) → ↑ strength and rigidity BUT ↓ diffusion kinetics (slower exchange).
- ☐ ↑ Multivalent counter-ion → ↑ selectivity BUT harder to regenerate.

### Common-Sense Benchmarks
- ☐ Commercial SAC resin IEC ≈ **4.5–5.0 meq/g**.
- ☐ Optimum DS for fuel-cell SPEEK ≈ **30–50 %**.
- ☐ Optimum water uptake for PEMs ≈ **25–35 %**.
- ☐ Typical DVB crosslink content ≈ **4–10 %**.
- ☐ Working pH range for SAC = **0–14**; WAC = **5–14**; SBA = **0–14**; WBA = **0–7**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyelectrolytes Based on Carboxylates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT751 - SPECIALITY POLYMERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Polyelectrolytes Based on Carboxylates ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits inside **Module 2: Functional Polyelectrolytes & Specialty Conducting/Responsive Polymers**, and directly maps to **CO2** — *Describe the synthesis, structure–property relationships, and applications of specialty functional polymers.* ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **polyelectrolyte** is a macromolecule that contains a substantial number of ionizable or ionic functional groups along its backbone or as pendant side-chains. When dissolved in a polar solvent (typically water), these macromolecules **dissociate into a polycation or polyanion accompanied by low-molecular-weight counterions** (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{H}^+$), producing a *polymer electrolyte solution* capable of conducting electricity and exhibiting characteristic physico-chemical behaviour such as chain expansion, counterion condensation, and pH-responsiveness. **Polyelectrolytes Based on Carboxylates** are a sub-class of **anionic (poly-anionic) polyelectrolytes** in which the ionizable functional group is the **carboxylate anion** ($-\text{COO}^-$), generated from the neutralization or deprotonation of pendant carboxylic acid ($-\text{COOH}$) groups anchored to a polymer backbone. Representative polymers include **poly(acrylic acid) (PAA)**, **poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA)**, **salts thereof (sodium polyacrylate, sodium alginate)**, and **chemically modified polysaccharides such as carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Examiner-Standard Definition (memorizable):** > *"A carboxylate polyelectrolyte is a high-molecular-weight polymer carrying pendant $-\text{COO}^-$ groups, which, on contact with water, ionize to yield a polyanion and mobile counter-cations, thereby rendering the polymer water-soluble, pH-responsive, and electrically conductive."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **ball of yarn made of a long beaded necklace** where every few beads are replaced by tiny **magnets** ($\text{COO}^-$) carrying a negative charge. When this yarn is dropped into water, the magnets (carboxylates) repel each other because they share the same sign of charge. The yarn therefore **stretches out, swells, and tries to occupy as much space as possible** — exactly the opposite of a regular polymer in a good solvent, which would coil. - The **negative magnets** = carboxylate anions ($-\text{COO}^-$) - The **water molecules** = polar solvent shell that hydrates each carboxylate - The **little positive sodium ions floating around** = counterions ($\text{Na}^+$) that diffuse in solution - The **stretching yarn** = chain expansion and viscosity build-up If we **add acid (HCl)** to the water, the protons ($\text{H}^+$) bind to the carboxylates, converting them back to neutral $-\text{COOH}$. The magnets vanish, the yarn recoils, and the polymer **precipitates or collapses**. This reversible "magnet on / magnet off" switching is the very essence of **pH-responsiveness** that makes carboxylate polyelectrolytes indispensable in **drug delivery, superabsorbents, and smart coatings**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Degree of neutralization ($\alpha_n$):** dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha_n \le 1$, denoting the fraction of $-\text{COOH}$ groups converted to $-\text{COO}^-$. - **Linear charge density ($\xi$):** dimensionless Bjerrum-length-related parameter, used to predict counterion condensation (Manning theory threshold $\xi_{\text{crit}} = 1$ in water at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$). - **Counterion association constant ($\log K_a$):** typically $-\text{COOH}$ has $\text{p}K_a \approx 4.25$ for PAA and $\text{p}K_a \approx 5.5$ for PMAA. - **Characteristic ratio ($C_n$):** $C_n \approx 6.7$ for PAA, $C_n \approx 8.3$ for PMAA. - **Standard reference temperature:** **25 °C (298.15 K)** for ionization equilibria. ### 1.4 Classification Snapshot > [!TIP] > **Classify carboxylate polyelectrolytes by ORIGIN of the polymer chain:** | Origin Class | Examples | Repeat Unit Snippet | |---|---|---| | **Vinyl-addition (synthetic)** | Poly(acrylic acid), Poly(methacrylic acid) | $-\text{[CH}_2\text{-CH(COOH)]}_n\text{-}$ | | **Natural / Semi-synthetic** | Sodium alginate, Carboxymethyl cellulose, Hyaluronic acid, Pectin | Polysaccharide backbone bearing $-\text{COOH}$ or $-\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$ | | **Crosslinked networks** | Sodium polyacrylate hydrogels, Carbomers (Carbopol®) | Crosslinked PAA with $-\text{COO}^-\text{Na}^+$ | ### 1.5 Why Carboxylates? — Quick Differentiator Carboxylates are the **most industrially relevant** anionic polyelectrolyte family because: 1. **Mild $\text{p}K_a$** (4–6) ⇒ ionizes under mildly basic conditions — biologically and environmentally benign. 2. **High water solubility & swellability** of the neutralized salt form. 3. **Strong chelation** of divalent / trivalent cations ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Al}^{3+}$) — exploited in water softening. 4. **Reversible, biocompatible, FDA-approved** for food, pharma, and personal care. 5. **Inexpensive monomers** (acrylic acid, methacrylic acid) — bulk commodity production. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Dissociation Equilibrium of Pendant Carboxylic Groups Consider a single pendant $-\text{COOH}$ group on a polymer chain in aqueous medium. The equilibrium is: $$ -\text{COOH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; -\text{COO}^- + \text{H}^+ \qquad \text{(polymer-bound acid dissociation)} $$ The **apparent dissociation constant** $K_a^{\text{app}}$ for the polyacid is **shifted to higher values** compared to the corresponding monomer because once one group ionizes, the developing negative charge on the chain makes subsequent ionizations easier (polyelectrolyte effect). This is captured by the **Henderson–Hasselbalch formalism applied to polyacids**: $$ \text{pH} \;=\; \text{p}K_a^{\text{app}} \;+\; \log\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_n}{1 - \alpha_n}\right) $$ where $\alpha_n$ is the degree of neutralization. **Why it matters:** The titration curve of PAA is **broader and shifted** relative to acetic acid (which has $\text{p}K_a = 4.76$). KTU examiners frequently ask students to **explain this shift qualitatively** — the answer is the **electrostatic repulsion among accumulated $-\text{COO}^-$ groups** plus **entropic counterion release**. ### 2.2 Counterion Condensation — Manning–Oosawa Theory For a polyanion with linear charge density parameter $\xi$: $$ \xi \;=\; \dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T \, b} $$ where: - $e$ = elementary charge ($1.602\times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$) - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of water $\approx 78.5$ - $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant ($1.381\times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$) - $b$ = average distance between consecutive ionizable groups along the chain (in metres) - $T$ = **298.15 K** (standard) **Manning's Criterion:** When $\xi > 1$, **counterion condensation** occurs — a fraction of counterions "condense" on the polyanion to reduce the effective charge to the critical value $\xi = 1$. For PAA with $b \approx 0.252\,\text{nm}$, $\xi \approx 2.85$, hence **Manning condensation strongly applies** — explaining why even at high $\alpha_n$, a substantial fraction of $\text{Na}^+$ stays "bound" to the chain. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering implication:** Counterion condensation reduces **osmotic pressure**, **chain expansion**, and **solution conductivity** from theoretical maxima. It governs **drug-release kinetics** and **swelling pressure** in carboxylate hydrogels. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every high-yield equation for this topic. Use `\vert` (NOT the bare pipe symbol) inside any cell that contains absolute-value or modulus notation, to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Polyacid Henderson–Hasselbalch** | $\text{pH}=\text{p}K_a^{\text{app}}+\log\!\big(\alpha_n/(1-\alpha_n)\big)$ | $\alpha_n$ dimensionless | pH–neutralization relationship | | 2 | **Bjerrum length** | $\lambda_B = e^2/(4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T)$ | $\lambda_B \approx 0.714\,\text{nm}$ in water | Distance at which two elementary charges interact with thermal energy $k_BT$ | | 3 | **Manning linear charge density** | $\xi=\lambda_B/b$ | $b$ = charge spacing (m) | Predicts counterion condensation when $\xi>1$ | | 4 | **Fraction of free counterions** | $f_{\text{free}}=1-(1/\xi)$ for $\xi>1$ | dimensionless | Mobile counterion fraction | | 5 | **Osmotic pressure (ideal Donnan)** | $\Pi = c_sRT$ | $c_s$ = salt concentration (mol/L) | Drives hydrogel swelling | | 6 | **Flory swelling ratio (PAA hydrogel)** | $Q^{5/3}\approx (V_u/\bar{v}\,v_2^2\,I)$ | $V_u$ = molar volume, $I$ = ionic strength | Predicts equilibrium water uptake | | 7 | **Mark–Houwink for PAA** | $[\eta]=K M_a^{\alpha}$ | $K$, $\alpha$ constants; $\alpha\approx 0.66$ in $0.1$ M NaCl | Intrinsic viscosity scaling | | 8 | **Carboxylate protonation constant** | $\text{p}K_a^{\text{app}}\approx \text{p}K_{a,0}+(0.434)\Delta G_{\text{elec}}/RT$ | $\Delta G_{\text{elec}}$ electrostatic free energy | Cooperative ionization | | 9 | **Critical salt concentration for salting-out** | $c_{\text{salt}}^{\text{crit}}\propto 1/\alpha_n$ | mol/L | Threshold above which polyelectrolyte precipitates | | 10 | **Glass transition shift (plasticization by water)** | $T_g(\text{wet})\approx T_g(\text{dry}) - k\cdot w$ | $w$ = water mass fraction | Water acts as plasticizer in carboxylate films | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** "**M**anning requires $\xi>1$, **F**lory needs $Q^{5/3}$, **H**enderson sets the pH" — covers the three pillars of carboxylate polyelectrolyte theory. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Superabsorbent polymers (SAPs):** Sodium polyacrylate in diapers absorbs **≈300 g water per g polymer** through Donnan-osmotic swelling. - **Concrete admixtures (PCEs):** Polycarboxylate ether superplasticizers disperse cement by **steric + electrostatic repulsion** from $-\text{COO}^-$ side chains. - **Water treatment:** Sodium alginate and CMC bind heavy-metal cations ($\text{Pb}^{2+}$, $\text{Cd}^{2+}$) via chelation. - **Pharmaceuticals:** PMAA-based enteric coatings dissolve only above pH 6, protecting acid-labile drugs through the stomach. - **Cosmetics & food:** Carbopol® gives transparent, shear-thinning gels at concentrations as low as $0.1\,\text{wt}\%$. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Polyelectrolyte Dissociation Constant from Thermodynamic First Principles We start from the Gibbs free-energy change for the deprotonation of a single $-\text{COOH}$ group on a polyanion already carrying $i-1$ ionized sites. **Step 1 — Write the standard chemical potential contributions:** The free-energy change to ionize the $i^{\text{th}}$ site is $$ \Delta G_i^{\text{tot}} \;=\; \Delta G_i^{0} \;+\; \Delta G_i^{\text{elec}} \;+\; \Delta G_i^{\text{conf}} $$ where - $\Delta G_i^{0}$ = intrinsic dissociation free energy (same as the monomer), - $\Delta G_i^{\text{elec}}$ = electrostatic work to place charge $-(i-1)e$ on the chain against the field of already-ionized sites, - $\Delta G_i^{\text{conf}}$ = configurational entropy of the polyanion (often absorbed into $\Delta G_i^{0}$). **Step 2 — Express the apparent $K_a^{(i)}$ via Boltzmann:** $$ K_a^{(i)} \;=\; K_{a,0}\cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G_i^{\text{elec}}}{RT}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Use the Debye–Hückel approximation for $\Delta G_i^{\text{elec}}$ in a salt-free solution:** $$ \Delta G_i^{\text{elec}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{(i-1)e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\,R_{\text{rms}}} $$ where $R_{\text{rms}}$ is the root-mean-square end-to-end distance of the polyanion, approximated by a Gaussian coil: $$ R_{\text{rms}} \;\approx\; b\,\sqrt{2\,C_n\,n} $$ with $n$ = degree of polymerization and $C_n$ = characteristic ratio. **Step 4 — Combine and take logarithms:** $$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_a^{(i)}}{K_{a,0}}\right) \;=\; -\dfrac{(i-1)e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\,R_{\text{rms}}\,RT} $$ **Step 5 — Define the cooperative parameter and reach the final compact form:** Let $\Delta(\text{p}K_a) = \text{p}K_a^{(i)} - \text{p}K_{a,0}$. Then: $$ \Delta(\text{p}K_a) \;=\; \dfrac{0.434\,(i-1)e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\,R_{\text{rms}}\,RT} $$ Substituting numerical constants at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ in water: $$ \Delta(\text{p}K_a) \;\approx\; \dfrac{1.39\times 10^{-18}\,(i-1)}{R_{\text{rms}}}\, \text{J}^{-1} $$ This shows that **each successive ionization requires a slightly lower pH** because the polyanion is already negatively charged, *broadening* the titration curve — the well-known **polyelectrolyte effect**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Equilibrium Swelling Degree $Q$ of a Crosslinked Carboxylate Hydrogel **Step 1 — Total osmotic pressure in the gel (Flory–Rehner + Donnan):** At swelling equilibrium, three contributions balance: $$ \Pi_{\text{total}} \;=\; \Pi_{\text{mix}} \;+\; \Pi_{\text{elast}} \;+\; \Pi_{\text{ion}} \;=\; 0 $$ **Step 2 — Mixing term (Flory–Huggins):** $$ \Pi_{\text{mix}} \;=\; -\dfrac{RT}{V_1}\!\left[\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi\phi_2^{\,2}\right] $$ where $V_1$ = molar volume of solvent, $\phi_2$ = polymer volume fraction in gel, $\chi$ = Flory interaction parameter. **Step 3 — Elastic-retractive term (affine network model):** $$ \Pi_{\text{elast}} \;=\; -RT\,\dfrac{v_e}{V_0}\,V_1\,\phi_2^{1/3} $$ where $v_e$ = moles of elastically active chains, $V_0$ = dry polymer volume. **Step 4 — Ionic (Donnan) term — the carboxylate-specific driver:** $$ \Pi_{\text{ion}} \;=\; RT\!\left(\sqrt{(c_{\text{fix}})^2 + 4c_s^{\,2}} - 2c_s\right) $$ with $c_{\text{fix}}$ = fixed charge concentration (mol/L) from $-\text{COO}^-\text{Na}^+$. **Step 5 — Express swelling ratio $Q = 1/\phi_2$ and simplify in the high-swelling limit ($\phi_2 \ll 1$):** $$ Q^{5/3} \;\approx\; \dfrac{V_1\,v_e}{V_0}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2} - \chi\right)^{-1} \!\cdot\! \dfrac{c_{\text{fix}}^{\,2}}{\bar{v}\,c_s} $$ **Interpretation:** As $c_{\text{fix}}$ rises (more carboxylate groups ionized) and $c_s$ (external salt) drops, $Q$ grows. Adding salt **shields** charges and **collapses** the gel — the classical "**antipolyelectrolyte effect**". --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Synthesis Routes for the Three Major Carboxylate Polyelectrolytes > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently pose "compare the synthesis of PAA, PMAA, and sodium alginate" type questions. This matrix is your examiner-ready checklist. | Feature | Poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) | Poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) | Sodium Alginate | |---|---|---|---| | **Monomer** | Acrylic acid $\text{CH}_2\text{=CHCOOH}$ | Methacrylic acid $\text{CH}_2\text{=C(CH}_3\text{)COOH}$ | Alginic acid (extracted from brown seaweed) | | **Polymerization** | Free-radical solution polymerization in water; AIBN/APS initiator; $60{-}80\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Free-radical, often at lower T to avoid decarboxylation | **NOT polymerized** — natural polymer; converted to Na-salt by neutralization | | **Reaction (schematic)** | $n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CHCOOH}\xrightarrow{\text{R}^{\bullet}}-\text{[CH}_2\text{-CH(COOH)]}_n\text{-}$ | $n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=C(CH}_3\text{)COOH}\xrightarrow{\text{R}^{\bullet}}-\text{[CH}_2\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)(COOH)]}_n\text{-}$ | $\text{Alginic{-}COOH}+\text{NaOH}\rightarrow\text{Alginate{-}COO}^-\text{Na}^++\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | | **Molecular weight** | $M_w \approx 10^4 {-} 10^6\,\text{g/mol}$ (controllable by CTA) | $M_w \approx 10^4 {-} 5\times 10^5\,\text{g/mol}$ | $M_w \approx 1{-}5\times 10^5\,\text{g/mol}$ | | **$\text{p}K_a$ (apparent)** | $\approx 4.25$ | $\approx 5.5$ (more hydrophobic → suppressed ionization) | $\approx 3.4$ (guluronic) and $\approx 3.7$ (mannuronic) blocks | | **Glass transition $T_g$** | $\approx 106\,^\circ\text{C}$ (dry) | $\approx 130\,^\circ\text{C}$ (dry) | Non-thermoplastic (degrades before $T_g$) | | **Biocompatibility** | Good (but residual monomer issues) | Good | Excellent (FDA-approved for food) | | **Key application** | Superabsorbents, thickeners | Enteric coatings, pH-gated sensors | Wound dressings, edible films, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ gelation | --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Calculation > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A 1.0 L solution contains $0.05$ mol of PAA repeat units ($\text{p}K_a^{\text{app}} = 4.85$). The pH is adjusted to $6.50$. Compute (a) the degree of neutralization $\alpha_n$, (b) the number of ionized $-\text{COO}^-$ groups, (c) the approximate free counterion fraction using Manning theory given $b = 0.252\,\text{nm}$. **Solution:** **(a)** Apply Henderson–Hasselbalch: $$ \text{pH} - \text{p}K_a^{\text{app}} \;=\; \log\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_n}{1-\alpha_n}\right) $$ $$ 6.50 - 4.85 \;=\; 1.65 \;=\; \log\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_n}{1-\alpha_n}\right) $$ Take the antilog: $$ \dfrac{\alpha_n}{1-\alpha_n} \;=\; 10^{1.65} \;\approx\; 44.67 $$ Solve: $$ \alpha_n \;=\; \dfrac{44.67}{1 + 44.67} \;\approx\; 0.9781 $$ **(b)** Number of ionized groups: $$ N_{\text{ion}} \;=\; \alpha_n \times N_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0.9781 \times 0.05 \times 6.022\times 10^{23} $$ $$ N_{\text{ion}} \;\approx\; 2.945 \times 10^{22}\;\text{ groups} $$ **(c)** Manning parameter: $$ \xi \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda_B}{b} \;=\; \dfrac{0.714\,\text{nm}}{0.252\,\text{nm}} \;\approx\; 2.83 $$ Since $\xi > 1$, counterions condense. Free fraction: $$ f_{\text{free}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{\xi} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.83} \;\approx\; 0.647 $$ **Engineering interpretation:** About **$65\%$** of $\text{Na}^+$ ions are *mobile*, providing osmotic swelling; the remaining $35\%$ are condensed on the chain, contributing to Donnan selectivity. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > All Mermaid diagrams below follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safety protocol: **alphanumeric node IDs**, **uppercase raw text inside double-quoted labels**, **no bold/italic tags inside node labels**, and **no reserved keywords as node names**. ### 4.1 Repeat-Unit Architecture of the Three Key Carboxylate Polyelectrolytes ```mermaid graph LR monomerA["ACRYLIC ACID MONOMER
CH2=CH-COOH"] monomerB["METHACRYLIC ACID MONOMER
CH2=C(CH3)-COOH"] monomerC["D-GLUCURONIC ACID UNIT
POLYSACCHARIDE BACKBONE"] polya["POLY ACRYLIC ACID REPEAT
-CH2-CH(COOH)-"] polyb["POLY METHACRYLIC ACID REPEAT
-CH2-C(CH3)(COOH)-"] polyc["CARBOXYMETHYL CELLULOSE UNIT
BACKBONE-O-CH2-COOH"] iona["PAA SODIUM SALT
-CH2-CH(COO-NA+)-"] ionb["PMAA SODIUM SALT
-CH2-C(CH3)(COO-NA+)-"] ionc["CMC SODIUM SALT
BACKBONE-O-CH2-COO-NA+"] app1["SUPERABSORBENT DIAPER CORE"] app2["ENTERIC DRUG COATING PH 6"] app3["FOOD THICKENER PHARM BINDER"] monomerA --> polya --> iona --> app1 monomerB --> polyb --> ionb --> app2 monomerC --> polyc --> ionc --> app3 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Crosslinked Carboxylate Superabsorbent Hydrogel ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["DRY POLYACRYLATE NETWORK"] nodeB["POLYMER BACKBONE CHAINS"] nodeC["PENDANT CARBOXYLATE ANIONS"] nodeD["SODIUM COUNTERIONS MOBILE"] nodeE["SODIUM COUNTERIONS CONDENSED"] nodeF["COVALENT CROSS LINKER BIS ACRYLAMIDE"] nodeG["WATER MOLECULES INGRESS"] nodeH["DONNAN OSMOTIC SWELLING"] nodeI["ELASTIC NETWORK RETRACTION"] nodeJ["EQUILIBRIUM SWOLLEN GEL"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ ``` ### 4.3 pH-Responsive Conformational Switching of Carboxylate Chains ```mermaid graph LR lowpH["PH LESS THAN 4
DOMINANT FORM COOH"] coil["COMPACT COILED CONFORMATION
HYDROGEN BONDED DIMERS"] highpH["PH GREATER THAN 7
DOMINANT FORM COO MINUS"] rod["STRETCHED ROD LIKE CONFORMATION
CHARGED REPULSION"] midpH["PH NEAR PKA
PARTIAL IONIZATION"] blob["INTERMEDIATE BLOBBY COIL"] lowpH --> coil highpH --> rod midpH --> blob coil -.PH SWITCH.-> midpH blob -.PH SWITCH.-> rod rod -.PH SWITCH.-> midpH midpH -.PH SWITCH.-> coil ``` ### 4.4 Counterion Condensation Schematic (Manning) ```mermaid graph TD chainN["POLYANION BACKBONE
LINEAR DENSE NEGATIVE CHARGE"] bjerrum["BJERRUM LENGTH LAMBDA B 0.714 NM"] spacing["CHARGE SPACING B 0.252 NM"] xi["XI EQUALS LAMBDA B OVER B EQUALS 2.83"] condCase{"XI GREATER THAN ONE"} condYes["COUNTERION CONDENSATION OCCURS
F FRACTION CONDENSED EQUALS 1 MINUS 1 OVER XI"] condNo["NO CONDENSATION
F FREE EQUALS 1.0"] chainN --> spacing bjerrum --> xi spacing --> xi xi --> condCase condCase -->|YES| condYes condCase -->|NO| condNo ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions are aligned to KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Part A = 3 marks (≈60 words, no choice)**; **Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice, sub-parts a + b each 7 marks)**. Bloom's levels tagged in `[BL:x]`. ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a *polyelectrolyte*. With the help of a suitable example, distinguish between a polyacid and a polysalt. `[BL: Remember/Understand]` **Model Answer:** A polyelectrolyte is a macromolecule carrying ionizable functional groups which, in a polar solvent, dissociate into a poly-ion and oppositely charged low-molecular-weight counterions. - **Polyacid:** the unionized form bearing $-\text{COOH}$ groups (e.g., poly(acrylic acid) in acid medium). - **Polysalt:** the neutralized ionic form bearing $-\text{COO}^-\text{Na}^+$ groups (e.g., sodium polyacrylate). Conversion between the two is achieved by changing pH (acid–base neutralization). **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State **Manning's counterion-condensation criterion**. Why is it important for carboxylate polyelectrolytes such as PAA in aqueous media? `[BL: Understand/Apply]` **Model Answer:** Manning's criterion states that when the dimensionless linear charge density parameter $$ \xi = \dfrac{\lambda_B}{b} > 1 $$ counterions condense onto the polyanion, releasing their translational entropy and reducing the effective linear charge to $\xi_{\text{eff}} = 1$. For PAA, $b \approx 0.252\,\text{nm}$ and $\lambda_B \approx 0.714\,\text{nm}$, giving $\xi \approx 2.83 > 1$. Hence, ≈ 65 % of $\text{Na}^+$ counterions are mobile and ≈ 35 % are condensed. This governs **swelling pressure, conductivity, and Donnan selectivity** in carboxylate hydrogels and ion-exchange resins. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With suitable reaction schemes, describe the free-radical synthesis of **poly(acrylic acid) (PAA)** and **sodium polyacrylate**. Discuss the role of initiator, temperature, and crosslinker (if a hydrogel is desired). **[(7 marks), BL: Apply/Analyse]** **Model Solution:** **Synthesis of PAA (free-radical solution polymerization):** $$ n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CHCOOH} \;\xrightarrow[\;60{-}80\,^\circ\text{C}\;]{\text{AIBN or APS}}\; -\text{[CH}_2\text{-CH(COOH)]}_n\text{-} $$ - **Monomer:** Acrylic acid, $5{-}30\,\text{wt}\%$ in water. - **Initiator:** AIBN (for organic medium) or ammonium persulfate (APS, for aqueous medium), $0.1{-}1\,\text{mol}\%$ relative to monomer. - **Temperature:** $60{-}80\,^\circ\text{C}$ balances reasonable rate with low chain transfer. - **Inert atmosphere:** $\text{N}_2$ purging to exclude $\text{O}_2$ (a radical scavenger). - **Chain-transfer agent (CTA):** e.g., thioglycolic acid, used to control $M_n$. - **Neutralization step:** $$ -\text{[CH}_2\text{-CH(COOH)]}_n\text{-} \;+\; n\,\text{NaOH} \;\longrightarrow\; -\text{[CH}_2\text{-CH(COO}^-\text{Na}^+\text{)]}_n\text{-} \;+\; n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **For a superabsorbent hydrogel:** add $0.05{-}0.5\,\text{mol}\%$ **N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBA)** as a difunctional crosslinker; the resulting 3-D network can absorb $100{-}1000$ times its weight in water. **Key process variables summary:** - Higher initiator → lower $M_w$. - Higher temperature → faster but broader $M_w$ distribution. - Crosslinker density ↑ → swelling ↓, mechanical strength ↑. - Degree of neutralization ↑ → swelling ↑ (until counterion screening dominates). **(7 marks — 1 scheme + 1 initiator + 1 crosslinker discussion + 1 process variables table + 2 mechanism discussion + 1 applications link)** --- **(b)** Using the **Flory–Rehner theory**, derive an expression for the equilibrium swelling ratio $Q$ of a crosslinked PAA hydrogel immersed in saline solution. Explain, with quantitative reasoning, how the addition of NaCl affects $Q$. **[(7 marks), BL: Analyse/Evaluate]** **Model Solution:** At swelling equilibrium, total osmotic pressure = 0: $$ \Pi_{\text{mix}} + \Pi_{\text{elast}} + \Pi_{\text{ion}} = 0 $$ **Mixing term (Flory–Huggins):** $$ \Pi_{\text{mix}} \;=\; -\dfrac{RT}{V_1}\left[\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi\phi_2^{2}\right] $$ **Elastic term (affine network):** $$ \Pi_{\text{elast}} \;=\; -\nu_e\,RT\,V_1\,\phi_2^{1/3}/V_0 $$ **Ionic (Donnan) term:** $$ \Pi_{\text{ion}} \;=\; RT\!\left(\sqrt{c_{\text{fix}}^{\,2}+4c_s^{\,2}}-2c_s\right) $$ **Equilibrium in the high-swelling limit ($\phi_2\ll1$):** $$ Q^{5/3} \;\approx\; \dfrac{V_1\,\nu_e}{V_0\,(0.5-\chi)}\cdot\dfrac{c_{\text{fix}}^{\,2}}{c_s} $$ where $Q = 1/\phi_2$ and $c_{\text{fix}} = \rho_p\,\alpha_n/M_0$ (fixed-charge conc.). **Effect of NaCl:** At low $c_s$ (pure water), $\Pi_{\text{ion}}$ is huge and $Q$ is maximum. As $c_s$ rises, $\Pi_{\text{ion}}$ approaches zero (Donnan screening), so $Q$ **decreases monotonically** — the **antipolyelectrolyte effect**. **Quantitative illustration:** If $c_s$ increases from $10^{-3}$ M to $10^{-1}$ M (100×), the ratio $c_{\text{fix}}^{\,2}/c_s$ drops 100-fold, so $Q^{5/3}$ drops 100×, i.e., $Q$ drops by a factor of $100^{3/5} \approx 15.85$. The gel shrinks dramatically. **Engineering consequence:** Superabsorbent diapers must be shielded from urine salts to retain swelling capacity. **(7 marks — 1 framework + 1 each term + 1 simplification + 1 NaCl effect with numbers + 1 application comment)** --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]** **(a)** Compare and contrast the structures, properties, and applications of **poly(acrylic acid) (PAA)**, **poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA)**, and **sodium alginate**. Use a comparative table and at least one reaction scheme. **[(7 marks), BL: Analyse]** **Model Solution:** | Feature | PAA | PMAA | Sodium Alginate | |---|---|---|---| | Repeat unit | $-\text{CH}_2\text{-CH(COOH)-}$ | $-\text{CH}_2\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)(COOH)-}$ | Polysaccharide with $-\text{COO}^-\text{Na}^+$ | | Synthesis | Radical polym. of acrylic acid | Radical polym. of methacrylic acid | Extracted from brown algae + NaOH neutralization | | $\text{p}K_a^{\text{app}}$ | $4.25$ | $5.5$ | $3.4{-}3.7$ | | $T_g$ (dry) | $106\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $130\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Decomposes | | Biodegradability | Partial | Partial | Excellent | | Key application | Diaper SAP | Enteric coating | Wound dressing, food thickener | **Reaction scheme (PAA):** $$ n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CHCOOH} \xrightarrow[\text{N}_2,\;65\,^\circ\text{C}]{\text{APS}} -\text{[CH}_2\text{-CH(COOH)]}_n\text{-} $$ **Distinguishing features to highlight:** 1. PMAA has the additional α-methyl group → hydrophobic hindrance → higher $\text{p}K_a$ and stronger **inter-chain hydrogen bonding**. 2. Sodium alginate is a **natural block copolymer** of mannuronate (M) and guluronate (G) — the GG-blocks bind $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ in the *egg-box* model for hydrogelation. 3. PAA is **synthetic and tunable**; alginate is **biocompatible but batch-variable**. **(7 marks — 1 comparative table + 1 reaction scheme + 3 distinguishing features with explanations + 2 application comments)** --- **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **counterion condensation** using Manning's theory. Apply it to a sodium polyacrylate chain with $b = 0.25\,\text{nm}$ in water at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ and compute (i) $\xi$, (ii) the fraction of condensed counterions, and (iii) the effective linear charge density. Discuss the consequences for solution viscosity. **[(7 marks), BL: Apply/Evaluate]** **Model Solution:** **Theory:** When two like charges are within the Bjerrum length $\lambda_B \approx 0.714\,\text{nm}$ in water at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$, their Coulombic repulsion exceeds $k_BT$. If the spacing $b$ between consecutive ionizable sites on a polyanion is smaller than $\lambda_B$, counterions condense onto the chain to restore $\xi_{\text{eff}} = 1$. **Calculations:** $$ \xi \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda_B}{b} \;=\; \dfrac{0.714\,\text{nm}}{0.25\,\text{nm}} \;=\; 2.856 $$ Since $\xi > 1$, condensation occurs. The fraction condensed is: $$ f_{\text{cond}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{\xi} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.856} \;=\; 0.650 $$ So ≈ 65 % of $\text{Na}^+$ ions are condensed on the chain. Effective linear charge density: $$ \xi_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 1.00 $$ **Consequences for viscosity:** 1. Condensed counterions **screen intra-chain repulsion** → chain contracts → lower hydrodynamic volume → **reduced intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta]$. 2. On adding excess salt, additional screening collapses the chain further. 3. Viscosity obeys: $$ [\eta] \;\propto\; M^{0.5} \quad (\theta\text{-like}) \;\longrightarrow\; [\eta] \;\propto\; M^{0.8{-}1.0} \quad (\text{extended in salt-free}) $$ 4. Mark–Houwink exponent $\alpha$ shifts from ≈ 0.5 (screened) to ≈ 1.0 (salt-free), giving an empirical **"polyelectrolyte viscosity anomaly"** used to detect polyelectrolyte character experimentally. **(7 marks — 1 theory + 3 calculation steps + 2 viscosity consequences + 1 numerical link to Mark–Houwink)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in this topic — and how to avoid it:** > > 1. **Confusing intrinsic $K_a$ with apparent $K_a^{\text{app}}$.** Always write "$\text{p}K_a^{\text{app}}$" when discussing a polyacid — the monomer value differs by ~0.5–1 unit. **Lose up to 1 mark** for sloppy notation. > 2. **Forgetting the Bjerrum length value ($\lambda_B \approx 0.714\,\text{nm}$).** Examiners expect you to *state* this constant, not skip it. **Lose up to 1 mark.** > 3. **Mixing up PAA and PMAA.** PMAA has the α-methyl group and a *higher* $\text{p}K_a$. Confusing them in titration-curve questions costs a full sub-part. > 4. **Omitting units in Flory–Rehner derivations.** Always carry units through; show that $Q$ is dimensionless and $c_{\text{fix}}$ is mol/L. > 5. **Skipping the crosslinker discussion in hydrogel questions.** Even if not asked explicitly, mentioning MBA / N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) earns a bonus point. > 6. **Missing the antipolyelectrolyte (salt-shrinking) effect.** This is a *favourite* 2-mark sub-question. Always discuss it explicitly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Carboxylate Polyelectrolytes** - **Definition:** Polymers with pendant $-\text{COOH}$ / $-\text{COO}^-$ that dissociate in water → polyanion + counter-cation. - **Three flagship members:** PAA, PMAA, sodium alginate / CMC. - **Ionization governed by:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a^{\text{app}} + \log[\alpha_n/(1-\alpha_n)]$. - **Manning's criterion:** $\xi = \lambda_B / b > 1$ ⇒ counterion condensation; $f_{\text{cond}} = 1 - 1/\xi$. - **Bjerrum length in water at 25 °C:** $\lambda_B \approx \mathbf{0.714\,\text{nm}}$. - **Swelling driver:** Donnan osmotic pressure from fixed $-\text{COO}^-$ charges; balanced by elastic retraction of crosslinks. - **Antipolyelectrolyte effect:** adding NaCl collapses the gel by screening Donnan potential. - **pH-responsiveness:** collapse at low pH (–COOH), swell at high pH (–COO⁻). - **Key synthesis routes:** - PAA / PMAA → free-radical polymerization (AIBN / APS). - Sodium alginate / CMC → extraction + NaOH neutralization. - **Mark–Houwink for PAA:** $[\eta] = K M^{0.66}$ in 0.1 M NaCl; exponent rises toward 1.0 in salt-free water. - **Industrial applications:** superabsorbents (diapers), concrete superplasticizers, drug coatings, water treatment, food/pharma thickeners. - **Biocompatibility hierarchy:** sodium alginate ≈ CMC > PMAA > PAA (in that order, but all are FDA-cleared for specified uses). - **Crosslinker for hydrogels:** N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) at $0.05{-}0.5\,\text{mol}\%$. - **Examiner traps to avoid:** intrinsic vs. apparent $\text{p}K_a$; PAA vs. PMAA confusion; omission of $\lambda_B$ constant; missing the salt-shrinking effect. ---

topics.title: Polyelectrolyte Complexes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT751 - SPECIALITY POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polyelectrolyte Complexes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Polyelectrolyte Complex (PEC)** is a macromolecular assembly formed by the spontaneous associative interaction between two oppositely charged polyelectrolytes (a polyanion and a polycation) when brought into contact in an aqueous medium. The driving force for complexation is primarily the **entropically favorable liberation of low-molecular-weight counter-ions** that were previously bound to the charged sites on the polymer backbones, supplemented by Coulombic, hydrogen-bonding, and hydrophobic interactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Polyelectrolyte Complexes are classified under *Specialty Polymeric Materials* as a sub-class of **Functional/Responsive Polymers**. Examiners frequently test the *stoichiometry of complexation*, *pH-responsiveness*, and *ion-exchange behaviour*.

Mathematically, the simplest representation of PEC formation can be written as:

$$
\text{Polyanion}^{(n-)} + \text{Polycation}^{(m+)} \longrightarrow \text{PEC (Polysalt)} + (n+m)\, \text{Counter-ions (released)}
$$

Where $n$ and $m$ represent the number of ionic sites on each macromolecular chain. The complex is electrically neutral when the **equivalent ratio of cationic to anionic sites equals unity** (i.e., $\vert n \vert = \vert m \vert$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have two giant combs — one with all positive bristles and the other with all negative bristles. When you dip both into a bucket of water and shake them together, the bristles **"click" into each other** because opposite charges attract. As they snap together, the small salt ions that were originally stuck to each bristle (just to keep the combs from repelling themselves) get flung out into the water.

This is exactly what happens in a Polyelectrolyte Complex:

- **The combs** = long polymer chains (polyelectrolytes)
- **The bristles** = ionic groups (e.g., $\text{–COO}^-$, $\text{–NH}_3^+$)
- **The small salt ions** = $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$, etc.
- **The click** = the ionic crosslink that holds the complex together

The released counter-ions are the *thermodynamic "payment"* — Nature releases them to gain entropy, which is what actually drives the entire process forward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in industry:** PECs are water-processable, **solvent-free alternatives** to conventional polymer films and hydrogels, making them crucial in **green chemistry**, **pharmaceutical coatings**, and **medical device fabrication**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in PEC Characterization

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Charge Density | $\rho_c$ | $0.1 - 8.0 \,\text{meq/g}$ | Density of ionizable groups per unit mass |
| Equivalent Ratio | $Z$ | $0 < Z < 2$ | Ratio of cationic to anionic equivalents |
| Swelling Degree | $S_w$ | $50 - 500 \,\%$ | Water uptake at equilibrium |
| Conductivity | $\kappa$ | $10^{-6} - 10^{-2}\,\text{S/cm}$ | Indicator of free-ion mobility |
| Critical Salt Concentration | $C_s$ | $10^{-4} - 10^{-1}\,\text{M}$ | Salt level at which complex dissociates |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of PEC Formation — Step-by-Step Logic

The formation of a polyelectrolyte complex proceeds through a **cooperative, multistep process** rather than a single binding event. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *enumerate* these steps:

- **Step 1 — Chain Approach (Diffusion Layer):** The oppositely charged polyelectrolyte coils approach each other through Brownian motion. The diffuse double-layer around each chain starts to overlap, generating a local electrostatic potential gradient.

- **Step 2 — Counter-ion Release (Entropy Gain):** This is the *rate-determining thermodynamic step*. Condensed counter-ions (those held tightly in the Stern layer of each polyion) are released into the bulk solution. For a PEC formed from sodium polyacrylate and poly(allylamine hydrochloride), the reaction releases **Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions**.

$$
\Delta G_{\text{complex}} = \Delta H_{\text{electrostatic}} - T \Delta S_{\text{counter-ion release}}
$$

Since $\Delta S \gg 0$ (due to mixing of released ions), $\Delta G < 0$ even when $\Delta H$ is slightly positive (endothermic). The process is therefore **entropically driven**.

- **Step 3 — Ion-Pair Formation (Coulombic Bonding):** Direct anionic–cationic site pairing occurs. Each pair forms a strong ionic crosslink with a typical bond energy in the range of **$5 - 20 \,\text{kJ/mol}$** in aqueous media.

- **Step 4 — Cooperative Rearrangement:** Once a few ion pairs form, they bring neighboring sites into close proximity, lowering the activation energy for further pairing. This is **cooperativity**, mathematically expressed by a Hill-type cooperativity coefficient $n_H > 1$.

- **Step 5 — Phase Separation / Precipitation:** When the chain segments are sufficiently crosslinked, the complex becomes insoluble and **phase-separates** from solution as a coacervate (liquid droplet) or solid precipitate.

## 2.2 Classification of Polyelectrolyte Complexes

PECs are broadly classified by KTU-approved terminology into two principal families:

### 2.2.1 Stoichiometric PECs
- Formed at the **1:1 equivalent ratio** ($Z = 1$).
- Electrically neutral.
- Insoluble in water and most organic solvents.
- High density of interchain ionic crosslinks → mechanically tough.

### 2.2.2 Non-Stoichiometric PECs
- Formed when one polyelectrolyte is in **excess** ($Z \neq 1$).
- Carries a **net residual charge** of the polyelectrolyte present in excess.
- Soluble in water due to the net charge providing colloidal stabilization.
- Used in **layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly** for thin-film fabrication.

## 2.3 Factors Influencing Complexation

| Factor | Effect on PEC | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **pH** | Controls degree of ionization of weak polyacids/bases | Used for pH-triggered drug release |
| **Ionic Strength** | High salt screens electrostatic attraction → dissociation | Determines *in vivo* stability of PECs |
| **Charge Density** | Higher $\rho_c$ → stronger, denser complex | Tunes membrane permeability |
| **Molecular Weight** | Higher $M_w$ → less soluble, more stable complex | Affects film mechanical strength |
| **Mixing Order** | Addition sequence affects non-stoichiometric composition | Critical for LbL nanofilm uniformity |
| **Temperature** | Weak effect on Coulombic interactions | Influences coacervation vs precipitation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \dfrac{n_{+}}{n_{-}}$ | Equivalent ratio (cationic/anionic sites) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho_c = \dfrac{N_{\text{ionic}}}{M_w}$ | Charge density | eq/g or meq/g |
| 3 | $\Delta G = -T \Delta S_{\text{ions}} + \Delta H_{\text{el}}$ | Free energy of complexation | J/mol |
| 4 | $S_w = \dfrac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$ | Swelling percentage | % |
| 5 | $\kappa = \kappa_0 \, e^{-\alpha C_s}$ | Conductivity vs salt concentration | S/cm |
| 6 | $n_H = \dfrac{d \log(Y/(1-Y))}{d \log[L]}$ | Hill cooperativity coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_s^* = f(\rho_c, M_w)$ | Critical salt concentration for dissociation | mol/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The formula $Z = n_{+}/n_{-}$ is the *master ratio* in PEC design. A formulation chemist at a pharmaceutical company will adjust $Z$ deliberately to either precipitate a stoichiometric capsule (for sustained release) or to maintain a soluble non-stoichiometric complex (for injectable polyplexes in gene therapy).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Ratio $Z$ for a Formulation Mix

**Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):**
A polyelectrolyte complex is to be prepared by mixing a **sodium salt of poly(acrylic acid) (NaPAA)** with a **poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDADMAC)** solution.

Given:
- Mass of NaPAA used: $m_a = 2.00\,\text{g}$
- Molar mass of repeat unit of NaPAA: $M_a = 94.05\,\text{g/mol}$
- Mass of PDADMAC used: $m_c = 1.50\,\text{g}$
- Molar mass of repeat unit of PDADMAC: $M_c = 161.67\,\text{g/mol}$
- Each NaPAA repeat unit carries **one** anionic site ($\text{–COO}^- \text{Na}^+$).
- Each PDADMAC repeat unit carries **one** cationic site ($\text{–N}^+(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{–}$).

Calculate the equivalent ratio $Z$ and predict whether the resulting PEC is stoichiometric, polyanion-rich, or polycation-rich.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate moles of anionic repeat units (polyanion).**

$$
n_{-} = \frac{m_a}{M_a} = \frac{2.00\,\text{g}}{94.05\,\text{g/mol}}
$$

$$
n_{-} = 0.02127\,\text{mol} = 21.27 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate moles of cationic repeat units (polycation).**

$$
n_{+} = \frac{m_c}{M_c} = \frac{1.50\,\text{g}}{161.67\,\text{g/mol}}
$$

$$
n_{+} = 0.009278\,\text{mol} = 9.278 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the equivalent ratio $Z$.**

$$
Z = \frac{n_{+}}{n_{-}} = \frac{9.278 \times 10^{-3}}{21.27 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Z = 0.4362
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

Since $Z < 1$, the polyanion is in **excess**. The complex will be **polyanion-rich** (i.e., a non-stoichiometric PEC) and will therefore carry a **net negative charge** in aqueous suspension. It will remain water-soluble, suitable for coating applications requiring a negative surface charge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Marking Note:** Examiners award full marks only when the *units of every quantity* and the *direction of the conclusion* (stoichiometric / non-stoichiometric) are explicitly stated. Students who calculate $Z$ but skip Step 4 typically lose **2 of 7 marks**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Mass of Dry PEC Obtained

Continuing the same problem, the dry PEC mass can be predicted by applying the principle of **charge neutrality within the complex** (each ionic crosslink consumes one cation and one anion). Excess polyanion, if any, is washed out.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the limiting polyion.**

Since $Z = 0.4362 < 1$, the polycation (PDADMAC) is the **limiting reagent**; the entire $9.278 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol}$ will be incorporated into the complex.

**Step 2: Compute the mass of anionic repeat units consumed.**

By charge equivalence, the moles of anionic units consumed equals $n_{+}$:

$$
n_{-,\text{consumed}} = n_{+} = 9.278 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the corresponding mass of consumed NaPAA segments.**

$$
m_{a,\text{consumed}} = n_{-, \text{consumed}} \times M_a
$$

$$
m_{a,\text{consumed}} = 9.278 \times 10^{-3} \times 94.05
$$

$$
m_{a,\text{consumed}} = 0.8726\,\text{g}
$$

**Step 4: Sum the masses to obtain theoretical dry PEC mass.**

$$
m_{\text{PEC,dry}} = m_{a,\text{consumed}} + m_c
$$

$$
m_{\text{PEC,dry}} = 0.8726 + 1.50 = 2.3726\,\text{g}
$$

**Step 5: Account for the counter-ions released into the wash water.**

Each ionic crosslink releases one $\text{Na}^+$ and one $\text{Cl}^-$:

$$
n_{\text{salt}} = 9.278 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{NaCl released}} = n_{\text{salt}} \times M_{\text{NaCl}} = 9.278 \times 10^{-3} \times 58.44
$$

$$
m_{\text{NaCl released}} = 0.5422\,\text{g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The released NaCl (≈ 0.54 g) must be removed by **dialysis** before the dry PEC mass is weighed. KTU questions often include this dialysis-step reasoning for full credit.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for PEC Formulation

| $Z$ Value | Complex Type | Net Charge | Solubility | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Z \to 0$ | Polyanion-rich PEC | Highly negative | Soluble | Anionic flocculant, DNA mimic |
| $Z = 0.3 - 0.7$ | Polyanion-rich (moderate) | Slightly negative | Colloidal | Drug microcapsule shell |
| $Z = 1$ | **Stoichiometric PEC** | Neutral | **Insoluble** | Membrane, film, fibre |
| $Z = 1.3 - 1.7$ | Polycation-rich (moderate) | Slightly positive | Colloidal | Gene delivery polyplex |
| $Z \to \infty$ | Polycation-rich PEC | Highly positive | Soluble | Cationic flocculant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Mechanism Flow of PEC Formation

The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the sequential events from initial polyion mixing through to the final complex morphology. This diagram is a **board-exam favourite** in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Two oppositely charged polyelectrolyte solutions prepared in deionised water] --> B[Step B: Mix under controlled stirring at room temperature]
    B --> C[Step C: Chain approach and double-layer overlap occurs]
    C --> D[Step D: Counter-ions released to bulk solution Entropy gain]
    D --> E[Step E: Coulombic ion pair formation Crosslinking begins]
    E --> F{Step F: Check equivalent ratio Z}
    F -->|Z equals 1| G[Step G1: Stoichiometric PEC formed Neutral and insoluble]
    F -->|Z less than 1| H[Step G2: Polyanion rich PEC Net negative charge remains]
    F -->|Z greater than 1| I[Step G3: Polycation rich PEC Net positive charge remains]
    G --> J[Step H: Phase separation Precipitate or coacervate forms]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step I: Purify by dialysis to remove released salt ions]
    K --> L[Step J: Dry PEC obtained in powder film or hydrogel form]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The branch `F` represents the *decision point* governed by the equivalent ratio $Z$ derived in Section 3.1. KTU examiners appreciate students who verbally explain **why** each branch leads to its corresponding morphology.

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PEC Membrane

For membrane-based applications, the following block diagram maps the structure–property relationships of a typical PEC membrane.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        IN1[Polyanion Solution e.g. NaPAA]
        IN2[Polycation Solution e.g. PDADMAC]
    end
    IN1 --> MX[Mixing and Casting Unit]
    IN2 --> MX
    MX --> CS[Crosslinking Zone where ionic bonds form]
    CS --> DR[Drying Chamber Removes water and free counter ions]
    DR --> OUT1[Output: Dense Stoichiometric PEC Membrane]
    DR --> OUT2[Output: Porous Non Stoichiometric PEC Membrane]
    DR --> OUT3[Output: Hollow Fibre PEC Membrane for dialysis]
```

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Schematic of Layer-by-Layer (LbL) Assembly Using PEC Concept

A critical modern application of non-stoichiometric PECs is **Layer-by-Layer film fabrication**, used in drug-eluting stents and biosensors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Substrate e.g. glass slide is immersed in polyanion solution]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Rinsed with deionised water to remove weakly bound chains]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Substrate is immersed in polycation solution]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Rinsed again to remove excess chains]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: One bilayer has been deposited]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Repeat cycle n times to reach desired film thickness]
    S6 --> S7[Final LbL PEC Film with controlled nanoscale architecture]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a polyelectrolyte complex. Distinguish clearly between a stoichiometric and a non-stoichiometric PEC with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A polyelectrolyte complex (PEC) is a macromolecular assembly formed by the electrostatic association of oppositely charged polyelectrolytes in an aqueous medium, accompanied by the release of low-molecular-weight counter-ions.

- **Stoichiometric PEC** — formed when cationic and anionic sites are in a 1:1 equivalent ratio ($Z = 1$). The product is electrically neutral, water-insoluble, and dense. *Example:* sodium polyacrylate–chitosan complex at $Z = 1$.
- **Non-stoichiometric PEC** — formed when one polyelectrolyte is in excess ($Z \neq 1$). The product carries a net charge of the polyion in excess and is water-soluble. *Example:* PDADMAC–NaPAA at $Z = 1.5$ (polycation-rich, net positive).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the term *stoichiometric* with *covalent* crosslinking. Stoichiometric PECs are held by **ionic crosslinks only** — they dissociate in high-salt conditions, unlike covalently crosslinked networks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of counter-ion release in the thermodynamics of polyelectrolyte complex formation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Counter-ion release is the **primary thermodynamic driving force** for PEC formation. In solution, the charged sites of each polyelectrolyte are surrounded by condensed counter-ions (e.g., Na⁺ around a polyanion). When oppositely charged polyions associate, these counter-ions are expelled into the bulk solvent. This release produces a **large positive entropy change** ($\Delta S \gg 0$). The total free-energy change is

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H_{\text{el}} - T \Delta S_{\text{ions}}
$$

Because $T \Delta S$ dominates, $\Delta G < 0$ even when the enthalpy of direct ion pairing is weakly endothermic, making the process **entropically driven**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Stoichiometry and Properties*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A stoichiometric polyelectrolyte complex is to be prepared by mixing sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (NaCMC, $M_{\text{repeat}} = 222.16\,\text{g/mol}$, 1 anionic site per repeat) with poly(4-vinyl pyridinium bromide) (P4VPB, $M_{\text{repeat}} = 186.07\,\text{g/mol}$, 1 cationic site per repeat). Calculate the masses required to obtain **5.00 g of dry stoichiometric PEC** ($Z = 1$).

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss **three industrial applications** of polyelectrolyte complexes, clearly stating in each case whether a stoichiometric or non-stoichiometric PEC is preferred and the reason for that preference.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: For $Z = 1$, the moles of ionic sites must be equal.**

$$
n_{+} = n_{-} = n
$$

**Step 2: Express the total dry PEC mass in terms of $n$.**

$$
m_{\text{PEC}} = n \cdot M_{\text{NaCMC-repeat}} + n \cdot M_{\text{P4VPB-repeat}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
m_{\text{PEC}} = n \cdot 222.16 + n \cdot 186.07
$$

$$
m_{\text{PEC}} = n \cdot (408.23)\,\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $n$ given $m_{\text{PEC}} = 5.00\,\text{g}$.**

$$
n = \frac{5.00}{408.23} = 0.01225\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the individual masses.**

$$
m_{\text{NaCMC}} = n \cdot 222.16 = 0.01225 \times 222.16 = 2.722\,\text{g}
$$

$$
m_{\text{P4VPB}} = n \cdot 186.07 = 0.01225 \times 186.07 = 2.279\,\text{g}
$$

**Verification:** $2.722 + 2.279 = 5.001\,\text{g}$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Split:** 2 marks for setting up $n_+ = n_-$, 3 marks for algebraic manipulation, 1 mark for final numerical answer, 1 mark for unit and consistency check.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

| # | Application | Preferred PEC | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hemodialysis membranes** | Stoichiometric | Electrically neutral membrane avoids unwanted ion-exchange with blood; mechanical toughness is essential. |
| 2 | **Layer-by-layer (LbL) drug-eluting coatings** | Non-stoichiometric (alternating charges) | Net surface charge on each layer allows sequential deposition; thickness is controllable at the nanometre scale. |
| 3 | **Wastewater flocculation** | Non-stoichiometric (slightly charged) | Net charge neutralizes colloidal contaminants in water; the slight residual charge aids bridging flocculation. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Mechanism and Factors*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a stepwise mechanism, explain the formation of a polyelectrolyte complex. Highlight the **thermodynamic role of counter-ion release** in your answer.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss how the following factors influence PEC formation: **(i) pH of the medium, (ii) ionic strength, and (iii) charge density of the polyelectrolytes.**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The formation proceeds in **five cooperative steps**:

- **Step 1 — Diffusion and approach:** Polycation and polyanion chains diffuse and form an overlapping diffuse double layer.
- **Step 2 — Counter-ion liberation:** Condensed small ions (e.g., Na⁺, Cl⁻) are released into the bulk. Entropy rises.
- **Step 3 — Site pairing:** Direct anionic–cationic Coulombic ion pairs form, creating ionic crosslinks.
- **Step 4 — Cooperative growth:** Existing pairs bring neighbouring sites into proximity, accelerating further pairing (Hill coefficient $n_H > 1$).
- **Step 5 — Phase separation:** The crosslinked network phase-separates as a coacervate or precipitate.

**Thermodynamic justification:**

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H_{\text{el}} - T \Delta S_{\text{ions}}
$$

$\Delta H_{\text{el}}$ is small (often slightly positive in water because of solvation-shell rearrangement), but $T \Delta S_{\text{ions}}$ is large and positive. Therefore, $\Delta G < 0$ and the reaction is **entropically driven**.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Effect of pH (2 marks):** For weak polyelectrolytes, pH controls the degree of ionization. If pH is far from the $pK_a$ or $pK_b$ of either partner, one polyion may be incompletely charged, weakening the complex. PEC formation is therefore possible only within a **pH window** bracketed by both $pK_a$ and $pK_b$. This is the principle behind pH-responsive PEC capsules for **intestinal drug delivery**.

**(ii) Effect of ionic strength (2 marks):** Added salt (e.g., NaCl) introduces small ions that screen the electrostatic attraction between polyions, **decreasing the driving force** for complexation. Above a critical salt concentration $C_s^*$, the complex dissociates into its parent polyelectrolytes. KTU often uses this principle to test understanding of *in vivo* stability of injectable PECs (which encounter ~0.15 M NaCl in blood).

**(iii) Effect of charge density (3 marks):** Charge density $\rho_c$ determines the density of available ionic crosslinks. Higher $\rho_c$ produces:
- A **denser, less-swollen** PEC
- **Higher mechanical strength** but **lower permeability**
- **Greater salt resistance** (higher $C_s^*$ required for dissociation)

A $2\times$ increase in $\rho_c$ typically results in a $3 - 5\times$ increase in PEC shear modulus (empirically observed). This is exploited to tune the **permeability of PEC pervaporation membranes** for ethanol–water separation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. Students often **omit numerical units** in sub-part (a) derivations — loss of 1 mark.
> 2. In sub-part (b), students **list factors** without explaining the *direction* of the effect (i.e., "pH affects the complex" without saying *how*). Always state whether a factor *increases*, *decreases*, or *optimizes* the complex.
> 3. Avoid the trap of using the word "**covalent**" instead of "**ionic**" when describing crosslinks in a stoichiometric PEC. This is a **favourite trick question** in KTU boards.
> 4. Never confuse **coacervation** (liquid–liquid phase separation) with **precipitation** (solid formation). Both can occur but are different phenomena.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A polyelectrolyte complex (PEC) is an electrostatically driven assembly of oppositely charged polyions with concurrent counter-ion release.
- **Driving force:** *Entropic* — liberation of small counter-ions dominates over the small enthalpic contribution from direct Coulombic ion pairing.
- **Master formula:** $Z = n_{+}/n_{-}$ governs the composition, charge, and solubility of the resulting complex.
- **Stoichiometric PEC** ($Z = 1$): neutral, insoluble, dense, mechanically strong — used in **membranes and films**.
- **Non-stoichiometric PEC** ($Z \neq 1$): carries a net charge of the excess polyion, water-soluble, used in **LbL films, flocculants, polyplexes for gene therapy**.
- **Key salt parameter:** Critical salt concentration $C_s^*$ determines the stability window of a PEC in physiological media.
- **pH-responsiveness:** Weak polyelectrolyte-based PECs dissociate outside the pH window defined by $pK_a$ and $pK_b$.
- **Layer-by-Layer (LbL)** assembly is the modern industrial technique to fabricate ultrathin PEC films with nanometre-level thickness control.
- **Charge density $\rho_c$** is the most important *design parameter* in PEC engineering — it controls strength, swelling, and salt resistance.
- **Always specify units and the direction of effect** in any KTU answer to avoid losing 1–2 marks per sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymers with Novel Properties - Liquid Crystalline Polymers (LCPs): Concept of Liquid Crystalline (LC) Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT751 - SPECIALITY POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polymers with Novel Properties - Liquid Crystalline Polymers (LCPs): Concept of Liquid Crystalline (LC) Phase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid Crystalline (LC) Phase — Canonical Definition**
> A **Liquid Crystalline (LC) phase**, also termed a **mesophase** (Greek *mesos* = "in between"), is a unique state of soft matter that simultaneously exhibits **long-range orientational order** (characteristic of a crystalline solid) and **long-range translational disorder / fluidic flow** (characteristic of an isotropic liquid). It exists strictly between the conventional solid and isotropic liquid states of matter.

In polymer science, **Liquid Crystalline Polymers (LCPs)** are macromolecules whose repeat units contain **mesogenic moieties** — rigid, rod- or disc-shaped segments — that confer this dual solid-and-liquid character to the bulk material. The result is a polymer that **flows like a liquid during processing** (often at unusually low viscosities under shear) **yet retains anisotropic, crystal-like mechanical, optical, and dielectric properties in the solid state**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (CO Mapping — PEPOT751 Module 3)**
> After mastering this topic, the student must be able to:
> - **CO3 / Module-3 / Cognitive Level L2 (Understand):** Define the LC phase and discriminate it from isotropic melts and amorphous glasses.
> - **CO3 / Module-3 / Cognitive Level L4 (Analyze):** Classify mesophases based on order parameters and relate them to processing behavior.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making the LC Phase Click in 60 Seconds

Imagine a **crowded Kerala bus stand during Onam rush hour**.

| Phase | Real-world Analogy | Molecular Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Crystalline Solid** | A military parade — every soldier locked in exact 3D grid positions. | Molecules fixed in lattice: positional **AND** orientational order. |
| **Liquid Crystalline Phase** | A school assembly — children **all face the Principal** (orientational order), but can **slide past each other** anywhere in the hall (translational freedom). | Molecules share a common direction (the **director**, $\hat{n}$) but can move/translate freely. |
| **Isotropic Liquid** | A packed SBT bus at random — passengers point in random directions and tumble freely. | No order of any kind. Pure disorder. |

> [!TIP]
> The **director $\hat{n}$** is a unit vector that points along the average molecular orientation of the mesogens. Note that $\hat{n}$ and $-\hat{n}$ are physically equivalent (head-tail symmetry).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Order Metrics (Board-Exam Essentials)

The single most important scalar that quantifies "how crystalline a liquid crystal is" is the **Orientational Order Parameter**, denoted **$S$**.

$$S = \frac{1}{2}\langle 3\cos^{2}\theta - 1 \rangle$$

where:
- $\theta$ = instantaneous angle between the **long molecular axis** of a mesogen and the macroscopic **director $\hat{n}$**
- $\langle \cdots \rangle$ denotes the **ensemble average** over all molecules in the sample

**Reference values of $S$ (memorize for KTU board exams):**

| System State | Order Parameter $S$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Perfect single crystal | $S = 1$ | Perfect alignment (all $\theta = 0$) |
| Typical nematic LC (e.g., 5CB) | $S \approx 0.4 - 0.7$ | Moderate orientational order |
| Near clearing point ($T \to T_{c}$) | $S \to 0.3$ | Order fading toward isotropic |
| Isotropic liquid | $S = 0$ | Complete orientational disorder |
| Smectic phase (additional positional layering) | $S \approx 0.6 - 0.9$ | Order + layered translational order |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The clearing temperature ($T_{c}$ or $T_{NI}$)** is the temperature at which the LC phase transitions to an isotropic liquid and $S \to 0$. Below $T_{c}$, the phase is birefringent; above $T_{c}$, it is optically isotropic. This transition is the **defining fingerprint** of LC behavior.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical States of Soft Matter — Where LCs Fit

A polymeric LC lies in a precise thermodynamic window between order and disorder. The hierarchy is:

1. **Crystalline Solid** — Full 3D positional + orientational order. Elastic modulus $\sim 10^{10} - 10^{11}$ Pa.
2. **Liquid Crystalline (Mesophase)** — Orientational order retained (sometimes partial positional order); flows under shear.
3. **Amorphous / Glassy Polymer** — No long-range order of any kind; frozen liquid.
4. **Isotropic Liquid / Melt** — Zero order; viscosity governed by free-volume and entanglements.

---

## 2.2 Why Does the LC Phase Exist? — The Molecular "How" and "Why"

### 2.2.1 The Mesogen — The Molecular Engine
A **mesogen** is the rigid, anisotropic structural unit responsible for LC behavior. For polymers, mesogens are built into the chain as either:
- **Main-chain LCPs:** Mesogens strung along the polymer backbone (e.g., Vectra® — *p*-hydroxybenzoic acid / HNA copolyester).
- **Side-chain LCPs:** Mesogens grafted as pendants off the backbone (typically with a flexible *spacer* to decouple backbone and mesogen dynamics).

### 2.2.2 Geometric Anisotropy — The Onsager Criterion
The **Onsager excluded-volume theory** (1949) gives a beautiful physical explanation:

> A rod-like molecule of length $L$ and diameter $D$ forms an LC phase when its **aspect ratio** $x = L/D$ exceeds a critical threshold driven by concentration.

**Why it works (intuitive "Why"):** Long rods cannot randomly pack without creating large excluded volumes. Entropy is maximized when they align parallel to one another. This **competition between orientational entropy (favoring disorder) and packing / excluded-volume entropy (favoring alignment)** is the thermodynamic driver of the mesophase.

### 2.2.3 Thermotropic vs Lyotropic
| Trigger | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermotropic LC** | Phase induced by **temperature change** | Poly(*p*-phenylene terephthalamide) — Kevlar® — in the nematic melt state |
| **Lyotropic LC** | Phase induced by **concentration in a solvent** | Poly(*p*-benzamide) in concentrated $\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4}$ (spinning of Kevlar fibers) |

---

## 2.3 Classification of Mesophases — The Core Taxonomy (Board-Exam Gold)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic:** **"N–S–C–C"** = Nematic, Smectic, Cholesteric, Columnar. Master these four with their order dimensionality and you will clear any LCP question.

### 2.3.1 Nematic Phase ($\text{N}$)
- **Order:** 1D orientational order only (no positional order).
- **Structure:** Mesogens point along $\hat{n}$ but centers-of-mass are randomly distributed like a liquid.
- **Viscosity:** Lowest of all LC phases — flows almost like water but with anisotropic properties.
- **Director field:** $\hat{n}(\vec{r})$ can vary in space, giving rise to **defects** (disclinations).

### 2.3.2 Smectic Phases ($\text{S}_{A}$, $\text{S}_{B}$, $\text{S}_{C}$, …)
- **Order:** 1D orientational + **1D positional** (layered).
- **Structure:** Mesogens arrange in equispaced layers; within each layer they are liquid-like.
- **Sub-types:**
  - $\text{S}_{A}$: Mesogens perpendicular to layer plane.
  - $\text{S}_{C}$: Mesogens tilted by angle $\alpha$ from layer normal (the **tilt angle**, a key KTU parameter).
  - $\text{S}_{B}$: Hexagonal in-plane packing (closer to a true crystal).

### 2.3.3 Cholesteric / Chiral Nematic ($\text{N}^{*}$)
- **Order:** Local nematic order, but $\hat{n}$ rotates helically along a perpendicular axis.
- **Helical pitch $P$:** Defines the wavelength of selectively reflected light (used in thermometers, decorative films).
- **Bragg condition:** $\lambda_{\text{reflected}} = \bar{n}\,P$, where $\bar{n}$ is the mean refractive index.

### 2.3.4 Columnar / Discotic Phase
- **Order:** Disc-shaped mesogens stack into columns; columns arrange in 2D lattice (hexagonal, rectangular).
- **Relevance:** Hexa-alkyl triphenylenes (used in organic electronics, OFETs, photovoltaic active layers).

---

## 2.4 Order Parameter — The Maier–Saupe Framework

The **Maier–Saupe mean-field theory** (1958, 1959) provides the standard closed-form description of the nematic-isotropic transition. The key postulate is the **orientational pseudo-potential** acting on each mesogen:

$$U(\theta) = -\varepsilon \, S \, P_{2}(\cos\theta)$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = Maier–Saupe interaction strength parameter (J per molecule)
- $S$ = macroscopic order parameter (the very quantity being self-consistently solved for)
- $P_{2}(\cos\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\langle 3\cos^{2}\theta - 1\rangle$ = **second Legendre polynomial**

### Self-Consistency Loop

The single-molecule probability distribution follows Boltzmann statistics:

$$f(\theta) = \frac{1}{Z}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon \, S \, P_{2}(\cos\theta)}{k_{B}T}\right)$$

The order parameter must equal its own definition averaged over $f(\theta)$:

$$S = \int_{0}^{\pi} P_{2}(\cos\theta)\,f(\theta)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta$$

This is solved numerically. The result gives the celebrated **first-order nematic-to-isotropic transition** with a discontinuous jump in $S$ at $T = T_{c}$ (typically $S(T_{c}^{-}) \approx 0.44$).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day survival sheet**. Every quantity is **board-markable**. CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE: All vertical bars in math use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never bare $\vert$ — to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Equation / Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units / Range | Where It Appears |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \tfrac{1}{2}\langle 3\cos^{2}\theta - 1\rangle$ | $\theta$ = angle to director $\hat{n}$ | $S \in [-0.5,\, 1]$ | Defining LC phases |
| 2 | $P_{2}(\cos\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}(3\cos^{2}\theta - 1)$ | Second Legendre polynomial | Dimensionless | Maier–Saupe theory |
